title: India’s AI-Powered Pendant That’s Shaping the Future of Wearable Tech
content:
In an era where artificial intelligence is transforming how we work, communicate, and live, NeoSapien emerges as a game-changer in wearable tech. Their flagship product, the Neo 1 pendant, caught national attention after a memorable pitch on Shark Tank India Season 4. Dubbed the “Second Brain”, this AI-powered wearable is designed to enhance memory, boost productivity, and improve emotional connections — all from a pendant around your neck.

With a blend of personal vision, cutting-edge technology, and emotional storytelling, NeoSapien is creating waves as the first AI-native wearable focused on mental productivity in India.

 The Founders Vision Behind NeoSapien

The minds behind NeoSapien are Dhananjay Yadav, an MBA graduate from Christ University (Gwalior), and Aryan Yadav, an IIT Madras alumnus (Indore). Cousins by relation, co-founders by mission — their journey into wearable tech was not born in a lab, but from a deep emotional experience.

Dhananjay lost a loved one due to memory-related issues. The trauma sparked a critical question: What if people had a companion that could help them remember what truly matters — thoughts, tasks, and conversations?

From this question, Dhananjay to build a tool that bridges human memory with machine learning emerged NeoSapien — headquartered at the Scaler Innovation Lab in Bengaluru — and its flagship product: Neo 1, a pendant that aims to function like a second brain.

What Is Neo 1? A Wearable That Thinks With You

Unlike conventional wearables that track physical health metrics, Neo 1 is built to enhance cognitive productivity and emotional awareness. The pendant works in real time, listening to your conversations (with consent), detecting important cues, and storing information that can later be surfaced as reminders, summaries, or actionable tasks via its companion mobile app.

Key Features:

  • Real-Time Conversation Tracking & Transcription
  • AI-Powered Summary Generation
  • Emotion Insight Detection (EI)
  • Task Creation from Voice Cues
  • End-to-End Encryption & Privacy Controls
  • Minimalist Design Optimized for Daily Wear

At its core lies the Second Brain OS — NeoSapien’s proprietary AI engine that powers voice interpretation, emotion detection, and context mapping. The result? A wearable that’s more than smart — it’s intuitive, emotionally aware, and productivity-first.

Shark Tank India: A Game-Changing Moment

NeoSapien’s pitch on Shark Tank India Season 4 stood out not just for its innovation, but for its vision. The founders asked for ₹80 lakh in exchange for 2.5% equity, valuing the company at ₹32 crore.

After detailed questioning from the Sharks — particularly around data privacy, hardware reliability, and commercial viability — only one offer came forward.

Final Deal:

  • ₹80 lakh for 4% equity
  • Lead Investor: Namita Thapar
  • Condition: Prioritize mental wellness as the primary use case within one year
  • Advisory Support: Anupam Mittal (informally)

Namita Thapar, known for her focus on healthcare innovation, saw potential in NeoSapien as a mental wellness companion—especially at a time when post-pandemic burnout and cognitive overload are common concerns among urban professionals and students.

Target Audience & Applications:

  • Knowledge Workers & Creators: Note-taking, task tracking, brainstorming capture
  • Students: Lecture summarization, time management
  • Mental Health Advocates: Emotion awareness, mood regulation
  • Professionals: Meeting insights, productivity tracking

Key Differentiators:

  • Emotion + Memory Hybrid: Unlike smartwatches, Neo 1 prioritizes emotional context and memory intelligence.
  • End-to-End AI Stack: The pendant is powered by in-house AI, reducing reliance on third-party APIs.
  • Wearable-First Design: Small, discreet, and daily-wearable — unlike bulky AR headsets or smart glasses.

Challenges Ahead:

  • Privacy Concerns: Recording conversations, even with encryption, requires trust and transparency.
  • Hardware Scalability: Producing durable, affordable devices at scale is capital- and process-intensive.
  • Battery Life & Device Heat: Compact AI wearables face real constraints on performance vs. power usage.
  • Adoption Barrier: Convincing users to adopt a new wearable that listens all day is a behavioral challenge.

Privacy & Criticism

Despite its innovation, NeoSapien faces valid skepticism from tech communities and Reddit users.

Key concerns include:

  • Unclear privacy policies
  • Cloud reliance with limited offline use
  • No benchmarks vs existing AI tools or wearables
  • Hardware challenges like battery life and durability

NeoSapien has responded with plans to:

  • Release public privacy audits
  • Conduct third-party hardware stress tests
  • Add user-controlled logging & deletion
  • Offer “offline mode” for selective usage

The real trust will come from public-facing privacy documentation and transparent testing.

The Road Ahead

NeoSapien has already launched the Neo 1 pendant on their website, priced at around ₹10,000. Backed by Shark Tank India, supported by Scaler, and linked to Panasonic’s Ignition Program and Google AI Academy, NeoSapien has ambitious plans:

  • Expand production of Neo 1 for early adopters
  • Roll out B2B partnerships (HR tech, coaching, education)
  • Add regional language support and smart summaries
  • Expand R&D for future hardware iterations
  • Release public privacy audits and detailed user documentation
  • Launch globally in tech-forward markets by late 2026

With the AI wearables market projected to hit USD 138.5 billion by 2029, NeoSapien aims to carve its niche by being India’s first AI-native personal companion device.

Final Thoughts

NeoSapien represents a compelling fusion of emotional intelligence, AI innovation, and wearable convenience. It reflects a growing global trend: cognitive technology that supports the human mind, not just the body.

But the road ahead will require more than innovation — it’s about earning user trust, refining the technology, and staying ahead of ethical concerns in AI, consistent performance will decide whether NeoSapien truly becomes the “Second Brain” for millions or just another ambitious tech idea.For now, all eyes are on the next steps — and how this Bengaluru-based startup shapes the future of intelligent wearables in India and beyond.

title: The Future Animations: The Startup Story of Animation Industry!
content:
Introduction to ‘The Future Animation’

The Future Animation, based in New Delhi, has rapidly emerged as a prominent name in the digital storytelling and animation industry. On Shark Tank India Season 4, The company showcased its creative expertise establishing itself as a versatile animation and video production platform. Specializing in high-quality Pixar-style animation, the startup offers a broad array of creative services designed to cater to the diverse needs of businesses. The focus is on building a successful animation business by investing in R&D and creating high-quality original content. The goal is to compete globally and enhance the Indian animation industry's impact. The company aims to address common issues faced by traditional animation studios, such as creative blocks and substandard quality.

Key Highlights of The Future Animation

Company Name

The Future Animation

Founders

Rohit Thakur and Anmol Pandey

Founded

2020

Location

New Delhi, Delhi

Industry

Animation and Post-production

Asked Investment

₹50 Lakhs for 2.5% equity

Asked Valuation

₹20 Crores

Final Deal

₹50 Lakhs for 10% equity

Final Valuation

₹5 Crores

Investing Shark

Peyush Bansal

The Shark Tank India Pitch

The founders presented their vision to the panel of sharks:

  • Vineeta Singh
  • Peyush Bansal
  • Anupam Mittal
  • Aman Gupta
  • Varun Dua

They sought ₹50 Lakhs for 2.5% equity, initially valuing the company at ₹20 Crores.

Shark Tank Deal Dynamics

Final investment details: -

  • Investment : ₹50 Lakhs
  • Equity: 10%
  • Valuation : ₹5 Crores
  • Lead Investor: Peyush Bansal

 Service Offerings and Expertise

  • Animation Services
  • The Future Animation creates high-quality Pixar-style animated videos, offering both 2D and 3D animation. They specialize in character design and bring stories to life through engaging animated narratives.
  • Video Production
  • The company produces a variety of video content, including corporate videos, promotional materials, sales presentations, demo videos, and viral videos tailored to capture audience attention.
  • Pre and Post-Production
  • They also provide comprehensive pre and post-production services, which include scriptwriting, storyboarding, motion graphics, video editing, and sound design to ensure the final product is polished and impactful.

Target Market and Industry Segments

Primary client categories:

  • Startups
  • Corporate businesses
  • Educational institutions
  • Marketing agencies
  • Digital content creators
  • Tech companies
  • E-learning platforms

Technological Capabilities

Key Technological Strengths:-

  • Cutting-edge animation tools
  • Superior rendering methods
  • Innovative storytelling techniques
  • Design aesthetics inspired by Pixar
  • Efficient and scalable production processes

Market Potential and Expansion Strategy

  • Potential expansion areas include venturing into digital content creation, allowing for a broader range of multimedia projects.
  • Expanding into international markets would open up new revenue streams and global opportunities.
  • Offering specialized animation services could cater to niche industries, while integrating new technologies would enhance production efficiency and creativity.
  • Investing in training and skill development would help foster talent and ensure a competitive edge in the ever-evolving industry.

Challenges and Strategic Future Plan

  • Key focus areas include building a strong portfolio that showcases diverse projects, while also developing distinctive storytelling methods that set the brand apart.
  • A commitment to investing in the latest technology will ensure top-tier production quality, while assembling a skilled animation team will drive innovation and creativity.
  • Exploring new and emerging digital platforms presents opportunities for growth and reaching wider audiences.
  • Plans to create original content like Disney and Marvel highlight an ambition to elevate Indian animation. This vision aims to establish a significant global presence in the industry.

Conclusion

In conclusion, The Future Animation is poised to make a lasting impact on India's digital storytelling landscape. With its blend of Pixar-inspired animation and innovative video production services, the company stands at the forefront of creativity and technology. The backing from Shark Tank India further strengthens its credibility and showcases its potential to revolutionize visual storytelling and digital content creation. As it continues to grow, The Future Animation is well-positioned to shape the future of animation, offering unique solutions for businesses and creators alike, while contributing significantly to the industry’s evolution.

title: EtherealX Raises $20.5 Mn Series A to Build India’s First Fully Reusable Medium-Lift Launch Vehicle
content:
Introduction

Bengaluru-based Ethereal Exploration Guild (EtherealX) has raised $20.5 million (₹170+ crore) in an oversubscribed Series A funding round, co-led by TDK Ventures and BIG Capital, with participation from Accel, Prosus, YourNest Venture Capital, BlueHill Capital, Campus Fund, and Riceberg Ventures. This milestone underscores the growing maturity of India’s spacetech ecosystem and sets the stage for EtherealX to achieve a bold technological vision: developing Razor Crest Mk-1, the country’s first fully reusable medium-lift launch vehicle. By targeting full-stage reusability, EtherealX positions itself as a potential challenger to global launch leaders such as SpaceX, while demonstrating that India can produce advanced, globally competitive launch technology.

Funding Round Details

The Series A round comes after EtherealX’s successful $5 million seed round in August 2024, led by YourNest Venture Capital, with participation from BIG Global Investments JSC, BlueHill Capital, Campus Fund, Golden Sparrow Ventures, and angel investors including Karan Bhagat, Yatin Shah (IIFL Wealth), Kiran Shetty, and Akhilesh Agarwal. With this Series A infusion, EtherealX’s valuation has increased 5.5x to $80.5 million, reflecting strong investor confidence in the startup’s technology and execution capabilities. The oversubscribed nature of the round also highlights the growing interest in India’s space startup ecosystem, particularly in companies pursuing deep reusability and medium-lift launch systems, a segment that has traditionally been dominated by global players.

Investors are attracted not only by the technical ambition but also by EtherealX’s rapid execution and demonstrated progress in developing proprietary engines and integrated launch systems in just a few years.

Founders and Vision

Founded in 2022 by Manu J. Nair (CEO), Shubhayu Sardar, and Prashanth Sharma, EtherealX brings together expertise in aerospace engineering, propulsion systems, and mission design. The founders have consistently emphasized deep reusability as the key to transforming orbital access economics.

Unlike incremental improvements in launch cost reduction, EtherealX aims to reuse both the booster and the upper stage, a technically challenging goal that, if achieved, could significantly reduce the cost per kilogram of payload to orbit. The startup’s approach combines rigorous in-house engineering with a long-term vision of establishing India as a globally competitive player in the medium-lift launch market.

Razor Crest Mk-1: India’s First Fully Reusable Medium-Lift Rocket

The flagship vehicle, Razor Crest Mk-1, represents the culmination of EtherealX’s efforts to develop a fully reusable launch platform capable of multiple orbital missions. Key features include:

  • Up to 24.8 tonnes to Low Earth Orbit (LEO) in expendable mode.
  • Around 8 tonnes to LEO in fully reusable configuration.
  • Capability to deliver payloads to Geosynchronous Transfer Orbit (GTO) and Trans-Lunar Injection (TLI).

Unlike SpaceX’s Falcon 9, which primarily reuses the first-stage booster, EtherealX targets reusability across both stages, enabling unprecedented operational efficiency and cost savings. The startup is building a scalable engine and stage configuration, which will allow clustered engines on both stages for redundancy and reliability. This system design ensures that even with one engine failure, the rocket can complete its mission safely.

Proprietary Engines and Technological Edge

EtherealX has developed two proprietary rocket engines:

Pegasus (Upper-Stage Reusable Engine):

  • 80 kN thrust, vacuum specific impulse (Isp) 323 s
  • Uses a Full Flow Segregated Cooling Cycle (FFSCC)
  • Features an in-house additively manufactured turbopump

Stallion (Booster Engine):

  • 1.2 MN thrust, gas-generator cycle
  • Sea-level specific impulse: 306 s

For Razor Crest Mk-1, nine Stallion engines will power the booster stage, while 15 Pegasus engines will drive the upper stage. This clustering approach ensures scalability, redundancy, and performance optimization—critical for both safety and mission success.

Infrastructure and Timelines

To support its ambitious roadmap, EtherealX operates Base 001, a rocket engine testing facility in Cuddalore, Tamil Nadu, focused on upper-stage engine qualification. The startup has also secured a 150-acre manufacturing and testing campus in Andhra Pradesh’s proposed Space City, expected to be operational by mid-2026. This facility will enable integrated engine and stage testing at scale, ensuring that the company can iterate rapidly and validate complex systems before commercial deployment.

Key milestones

  • Engine hot-fire tests for Stallion and clustered Pegasus engines: mid-2026.
  • Technology demonstration flight: Nov–Dec 2027.
  • Commercial launches: end of 2028.

These timelines indicate EtherealX’s commitment to rapid execution, a factor that has drawn investor confidence and positioned the company as a credible player in the global launch industry.

Market Opportunity and Commercial Traction

The global space economy is projected to reach $1.8 trillion by 2035, driven by satellite constellations, Earth observation, defense, and deep-space missions. There is a growing demand among satellite operators for flexible, cost-effective, and reliable launch services beyond what SpaceX currently provides.

EtherealX aims to lower orbital transport costs to $500–$1,000 per kilogram, with long-term pricing potentially ranging between $350 and $2,000 per kilogram depending on reuse cadence and configuration. The startup has already signed $130 million in launch MoUs with international customers, including Japan’s Space BD and Taiwan’s TASA, and maintains collaboration agreements with ISRO, IN-SPACe, and other global satellite operators.

Team and Growth

Currently employing 67 people, EtherealX plans to scale its workforce to around 90 employees as it ramps up manufacturing, testing, and integration. The team brings together a mix of aerospace engineers, propulsion specialists, and mission planners, ensuring technical depth across all areas of the launch system.

Startups like EtherealX exemplify India’s shift from component suppliers and small launchers to globally competitive, reusable launch systems, supporting the country’s vision of growing its space economy from $8 billion to $45 billion over the next decade.

Conclusion

EtherealX’s ambitious roadmap, proprietary engines, and strategic partnerships position it as a potential game-changer for India’s space ecosystem. With plans for demonstration flights in 2027 and commercial launches by 2028, the company is poised to redefine orbital access economics and strengthen India’s presence in the global launch market. Razor Crest Mk-1 could become a benchmark for reusable medium-lift rockets globally, showcasing India’s capability in NewSpace innovation.

title: Success Story "Flipkart" : From a Small Apartment Startup to India’s E-Commerce Powerhouse
content:
Introduction

Every transformative startup begins with a simple belief and Flipkart’s journey is a testament to how a bold idea, customer focus, and persistence can reshape an entire industry. Flipkart is an Indian e-commerce pioneer that transformed how millions of people shop online. Founded in 2007, the company grew from a small online bookstore operated from a Bengaluru apartment into a multi-category digital marketplace, setting benchmarks in trust, logistics, and customer experience. Today, Flipkart is one of India’s largest e-commerce platforms and continues to redefine digital retail.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

Flipkart was founded by Sachin Bansal and Binny Bansal, two former Amazon engineers who shared a vision of building a technology-driven retail platform in India. While working abroad, they observed the efficiency and scale of e-commerce and realized that India lacked a similar consumer-friendly solution.

The inspiration for Flipkart came from a simple observation: access to books and reliable retail options was limited, particularly outside India’s major cities. The founders believed that technology could bridge this gap, making products accessible to everyone, regardless of location. Starting with books allowed them to test the market with manageable inventory and logistics, laying the foundation for future expansion.

 

The Existing Problem

At the time, India’s e-commerce ecosystem faced several fundamental challenges:

  • Low consumer trust: Most people were wary of paying online due to fear of fraud or delayed delivery.
  • Poor logistics: Delivery networks were unreliable, especially in smaller towns.
  • Limited digital adoption: Online shopping was unfamiliar to most consumers, and payment infrastructure was still developing.

Existing solutions failed to address these issues. Many companies struggled to combine product selection, reliable delivery, and trust, leaving millions of potential customers underserved. Flipkart’s founders identified this gap as an opportunity to create a solution tailored specifically for Indian consumers.

 

The Solution

Flipkart built an end-to-end e-commerce platform designed to address India-specific challenges. Key innovations included:

  • Cash on Delivery (COD): Eliminated the fear of online payments, building customer trust and encouraging first-time users.
  • Easy returns and customer-friendly policies: Reduced risk for buyers and encouraged repeat purchases.
  • Proprietary logistics network (Ekart): Ensured faster and more reliable deliveries.
  • Marketplace model: Enabled sellers across India to reach a national audience.

By focusing on trust, convenience, and accessibility, Flipkart made online shopping simple, reliable, and appealing to a broad audience.

 

Early Challenges & Struggles

Flipkart’s early journey was anything but easy. The founders worked long hours, handling packaging, shipping, and customer service themselves. Funding was limited, and operational errors were frequent. The team also faced skepticism from investors, suppliers, and consumers who were unsure if e-commerce could work in India’s unique market.

Despite these challenges, Flipkart iterated quickly, learning from early mistakes. The founders personally monitored deliveries, engaged with customers, and continuously refined operations and website experience. This hands-on approach allowed the company to build credibility and improve trust, an essential factor for growth.

 

Growth & Key Milestones

Once customer trust was established, Flipkart experienced rapid growth. Key milestones include:

  • Expansion from books to electronics, fashion, home essentials, and lifestyle products.
  • Millions of active customers across India, including Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities.
  • Launch of The Big Billion Days, one of India’s largest annual e-commerce sales events.
  • Walmart’s majority stake acquisition in 2018, marking a historic milestone for Indian startups.

Flipkart also invested in data-driven personalization, improved supply chain infrastructure, and technology innovations that allowed it to scale operations while maintaining customer satisfaction.

 

Business Model & Strategy

Flipkart operates primarily as a marketplace, earning revenue through commissions from sellers, advertising, and value-added services.

Its strategic approach includes:

  • Customer-first policies to increase trust and retention.
  • Enabling sellers with tools, logistics, and analytics.
  • Expansion into underserved regions and consumer segments.
  • Technology-led personalization and marketing campaigns.

This strategy allowed Flipkart to grow rapidly while maintaining operational efficiency and strong unit economics.

 

Impact & Recognition

Flipkart has had a significant impact on India’s economy and society:

  • Empowered millions of sellers to reach a national audience.
  • Created large-scale employment opportunities in logistics, technology, and operations.
  • Influenced payment adoption and strengthened supply chains nationwide.
  • Inspired countless Indian entrepreneurs to build digital-first companies.

Flipkart has also received global recognition as a leading startup and remains a symbol of India’s growing technology ecosystem.

 

Current Status & Future Vision

Today, Flipkart is a major force in Indian e-commerce, continuing to innovate in logistics, AI-driven personalization, and inclusive growth. Its long-term vision is to democratize commerce further- enabling entrepreneurship, improving affordability, and connecting India’s next billion internet users.

 

Conclusion & Takeaway

Flipkart’s journey demonstrates that understanding local challenges, solving them with innovative solutions, and maintaining a customer-first approach can create lasting impact. From a small apartment startup to a national retail powerhouse, Flipkart proves that persistence, adaptability, and trust are the foundations of industry-defining success.

title: Shark Tank India–Featured Defence Dronetech Startup insideFPV Raises ₹6 Crore in Pre-Series A Funding
content:
Introduction

Gujarat-based defence drone technology startup insideFPV, which rose to national prominence after featuring on Shark Tank India, has raised ₹6 crore in a pre-Series A funding round led by venture capital firm GVFL. The funding comes at a pivotal time for the Indian defence and drone ecosystem, as the startup sharpens its focus on building indigenous, battlefield-ready unmanned aerial systems for India’s armed forces. With this fresh capital, insideFPV aims to accelerate product development, strengthen engineering depth, and scale manufacturing capabilities for defence-grade drone platforms.

 

Funding Details and Use of Capital

The GVFL-led pre-Series A round will be deployed across product development, engineering capability building, and manufacturing readiness. A significant portion of the capital will go toward testing and validation, expanding production capacity, and meeting working capital requirements for defence deployments.

In addition, insideFPV will invest in deployment support infrastructure, including operator training, spare parts availability, and rapid repair and maintenance capabilities. This end-to-end approach reflects the startup’s strategy of not just manufacturing drones, but delivering mission-ready systems suitable for real-world battlefield conditions and extreme environments.

 

Company Background and Founders

Founded in 2020 by Arth Chowdhary, Deyvant Bhardwaj, and Oshi Kumari, insideFPV was established to address the growing demand for high-performance FPV drones manufactured entirely in India. What began as a consumer-focused drone company has evolved into a multi-segment UAV manufacturer serving defence, surveillance, agriculture, commercial, and government clients. The company has registered its intellectual property in India and follows a strong Make-in-India philosophy, designing and manufacturing its drones domestically rather than relying on imports or copied designs.

 

Product Portfolio and Technology Differentiation

insideFPV’s product lineup includes FPV drones, kamikaze drones, surveillance platforms, and interceptor drones, purpose-built for defence and security applications. A major differentiator is its “Plug and Fly” FPV drone architecture, which allows users to deploy drones immediately after charging—eliminating complex setup processes common in traditional FPV systems.

The drones are designed to be compact, robust, and agile, with pricing starting at approximately ₹2,800, making them accessible across varied use cases. The company has also achieved a world record flight time of 141 minutes on a single charge, underscoring its engineering and battery optimization capabilities.

 

Business Model

insideFPV operates a multi-segment business model spanning consumer, enterprise, agriculture, and defence markets. While the consumer segment helps drive early adoption and brand visibility, the company’s long-term growth strategy is anchored in defence and government contracts, which offer higher order values, longer contract cycles, and recurring revenue through maintenance and support.

Revenue streams include:

  • Direct drone sales.
  • Defence and government contracts.
  • Enterprise and agricultural deployments.
  • Training, maintenance, and after-sales support.
  • Custom drone solutions for surveillance and security.

This diversified model allows insideFPV to balance scale, innovation, and sustainability, while gradually increasing its defence-sector exposure.

 

Traction, Recognition, and Shark Tank India

insideFPV became the first Indian drone company to appear on Shark Tank India (Season 2), where it raised ₹75 lakh for 15% equity from Peyush Bansal, Namita Thapar, Amit Jain, and Aman Gupta, valuing the company at ₹5 crore. The appearance significantly boosted brand awareness and credibility.

The startup has since won the Him-Dron-A-thon 2024, been featured in Forbes 30 Under 30 Asia 2024, and received the TIMES Most Promising Drone Tech Startup Award, cementing its position as a rising player in India’s UAV ecosystem.

 

Commercial Traction and Global Exposure

To date, insideFPV has served 1,000+ customers, delivered 2,000+ orders, registered over 15,000 potential buyers, and set up 10+ drone arenas across India. The company has also supplied drones to three defence regiments, marking early traction within the defence sector. On the international front, insideFPV has reportedly secured a long-term agreement to supply 10,000 drones to Israel over the next 10 years, signaling its ambitions to compete globally.

 

Future Plans

Over the next 12 months, insideFPV plans to:

  • Scale manufacturing capacity.
  • Expand its defence-focused product portfolio.
  • Strengthen testing, validation, and deployment capabilities.

Over the next three to five years, the startup aims to establish itself as a global defence drone manufacturer, serving international military and security agencies while playing a key role in India’s defence self-reliance mission. The company also plans to deepen collaborations with government bodies, defence institutions, and global partners.

 

Previous Funding and Investor Support

Prior to the GVFL-led round, insideFPV raised multiple seed rounds from Inflection Point Ventures, Mumbai Angels, Soonicorn, and angel investors including Fossbytes and Kumar Sahu. Overall, the startup has raised approximately $880K across six funding rounds, with its last reported valuation at $2.73 million (September 2023).

 

Conclusion

With strong investor backing, proven traction, and a clear defence-first roadmap, insideFPV is emerging as a serious contender in India’s rapidly evolving drone and defence technology landscape. As India accelerates its push toward indigenous defence manufacturing, startups like insideFPV could play a defining role in shaping the future of unmanned warfare and security systems.

title: Rapido Crosses ₹1,000 Crore Total Income in FY25 as Delivery Business Outpaces Ride-Hailing
content:
Introduction

Bengaluru-based mobility platform Rapido has crossed the ₹1,000 crore total income milestone in FY25, marking a significant inflection point in its journey toward sustainable scale. The Prosus-backed company reported 44% year-on-year growth in operating revenue while simultaneously narrowing its losses, signalling improving unit economics amid a challenging mobility landscape.

Rapido has remained in the spotlight over the past year after delivering strong returns to early investors such as TVS Motor, Swiggy, Prosus, Accel, and others. It also drew wider attention after Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi publicly acknowledged Rapido as a larger competitive threat in India. The latest financial performance reinforces Rapido’s transition from a high-growth challenger to a category leader in select mobility segments.

 

Financial Performance in FY25

According to consolidated financial statements filed with the Registrar of Companies (RoC), Rapido’s revenue from operations rose to ₹934 crore in FY25, up from ₹648 crore in FY24. Including other income of ₹69 crore, primarily from interest on investments, the company’s total income reached ₹1,003 crore, compared to ₹579 crore in the previous fiscal year.

Driven by strong revenue growth and controlled cost expansion, Rapido reduced its net loss by 30.5% to ₹258 crore in FY25, from ₹371 crore in FY24. The company’s EBITDA margin improved to -19.59%, while ROCE stood at -13.58%, indicating gradual progress toward operational sustainability.

 

Delivery Business Emerges as the Largest Revenue Stream

A key highlight of FY25 was the delivery business overtaking passenger ride-hailing as Rapido’s largest revenue contributor.

Revenue from delivery services, which includes food, grocery, and parcel transportation, grew 28.3% year-on-year to ₹340 crore, accounting for over 36% of operating revenue. In FY24, passenger transportation had been the dominant segment, but FY25 marked a decisive shift in the company’s revenue mix.

In contrast, platform-led passenger ride revenue, earned through commissions on two-, three-, and four-wheeler rides, declined 23.5% year-on-year to ₹277 crore, contributing around 29% of total operating revenue.

 

Subscription Model Drives Strong Growth

Rapido’s subscription-based monetization model emerged as one of the strongest growth drivers during FY25. Subscription income—collected from captains and users for ride passes and platform benefits—surged nearly 14X to ₹275 crore, contributing close to 30% of operating revenue.

Unlike competitors such as Ola and Uber, which primarily follow commission-based models, Rapido increasingly operates a SaaS-style subscription approach, enabling drivers to retain a higher share of earnings while offering riders fares that are 10–15% cheaper. This model has helped the company build strong supply-side loyalty and improve platform efficiency.

 

Cost Structure and Unit Economics

On the cost side, Rapido’s total expenses increased to ₹1,261 crore in FY25, from ₹1,066 crore in FY24.

  • Delivery charges and captain incentives remained the largest expense at ₹500 crore, accounting for nearly 40% of total costs.
  • Employee benefit expenses rose 20% year-on-year to ₹207 crore.
  • Advertising and promotional spend stood at ₹252 crore.
  • Research and development expenses amounted to ₹108 crore.

On a unit economics basis, Rapido spent ₹1.35 to earn every rupee of operating revenue, reflecting improving efficiency compared to previous years.

 

About the Founders

Founded in 2015, Rapido was started by Aravind Sanka, SR Rishikesh, and Pavan Guntupalli. CEO Aravind Sanka (IIT Bombay, ex-Flipkart) envisioned a two-wheeler-led solution to India’s urban commuting challenges. Pavan Guntupalli, also a co-founder of Ola, brought early experience in scaling ride-hailing platforms, while SR Rishikesh led operational execution and platform development.

 

Business Expansion and New Verticals

Founded in 2015 by Aravind Sanka, SR Rishikesh, and Pavan Guntupalli, Rapido has steadily expanded beyond its core bike-taxi offering.

Over the past two years, the company:

  • Entered four-wheeler cab services, competing with Ola, Uber, and Namma Yatri.
  • Launched Ownly, its food delivery platform aimed at challenging the Zomato–Swiggy duopoly.
  • Expanded parcel and last-mile logistics offerings.

Rapido also incorporated three new subsidiaries in 2025:

  • CtrlX Technologies Pvt Ltd, which operates Ownly.
  • Shyogsamart Technology Pvt Ltd, focused on financial lending services.
  • Flexiride Solutions Pvt Ltd, which operates the car and bike-pooling app Hopr.

 

Funding and Market Position

Rapido has raised over $550 million to date, including a $200 million unicorn round led by WestBridge Capital. The company has also completed multiple secondary transactions over the past year, providing liquidity to early investors and employees.

Rapido currently commands:

  • ~70% market share in bike taxis.
  • ~40% in auto rides.
  • ~22% in cab-hailing.

Its stronghold in two-wheeler mobility has helped open up a new market and forced regulatory and competitive shifts across India’s mobility ecosystem.

 

Future Plans & Outlook

While regulatory uncertainty continues to impact the passenger mobility segment, Rapido’s delivery and subscription-led revenue streams offer higher growth potential and better margins. Looking ahead, the company plans to expand to 500 cities, scale its food delivery pilot, onboard more electric two-wheelers, and potentially explore an IPO by late 2026.

Rapido’s FY25 performance signals a clear shift—from rapid expansion fueled by discounts to disciplined growth driven by sustainable unit economics.

 

Conclusion

Rapido’s FY25 performance signals a decisive shift—from growth fueled by heavy discounts to disciplined expansion driven by sustainable unit economics. As delivery and subscription revenues gain prominence, the company is steadily redefining India’s mobility and logistics playbook.

title: Truva Raises $9 Million in Fresh Capital Led by Stellaris and Orios Ventures
content:
Introduction

Indian resale homes platform Truva has raised $9 million (₹78 crore) in fresh funding, highlighting growing investor confidence in technology-led solutions that address inefficiencies in India’s fragmented secondary real estate market. The round was led by existing investors Stellaris Venture Partners and Orios Venture Partners, signaling continued belief in Truva’s full-stack, execution-driven approach to resale housing.

About the Funding

Of the total $9 million raised, $7.3 million (₹61 crore) was secured as equity, while $1.7 million (₹17 crore) came as venture debt from Stride Ventures. The round also attracted a group of prominent angel investors, including Mukesh Bansal (Co-founder, Myntra & CureFit), Aakrit Vaish and Miten Sampat (Co-founders, PayCheque), Ramakant Sharma (Co-founder, LivSpace), Natasha Malpani Oswal (Founder, Boundless Ventures), and Ram Raheja (MD, S Raheja Realty).

This funding follows Truva’s $3 million seed round in August 2024, also led by Stellaris Venture Partners, marking a significant step in the company’s scale-up journey.

Use of Funding

Truva plans to deploy the new capital across multiple strategic priorities. A substantial portion will strengthen TruIQ, the company’s proprietary real estate valuation and liquidity intelligence engine that underpins pricing accuracy and transaction speed.

Additionally, the funds will support inventory-linked working capital, allowing Truva to scale core operational processes, including legal and physical verification, property staging, selective renovations, and end-to-end transaction management. Part of the capital will also fuel geographic expansion into new metros such as Delhi NCR and Bengaluru, alongside deepening its presence in Mumbai.

About the Startup

Founded in 2023, Truva is a full-stack resale homes platform that aims to make the home buying and selling experience seamless, transparent, and predictable. Unlike traditional listing platforms, Truva positions itself as a transaction partner, managing the entire resale process end-to-end.

The startup works on exclusive seller mandates, giving it control over inventory quality, pricing transparency, and operational execution. By integrating technology with on-ground execution, Truva delivers faster sales, better pricing, and higher customer satisfaction.

About the Founders

Truva was founded by Puneet Arora, Monil Singhal, and Ankit Gupta, who observed a major gap in India’s resale housing market. Despite homes being among the most expensive purchases for families, the resale process remained fragmented, opaque, and stressful.

The founders aimed to bring honesty, transparency, and predictability to resale transactions by combining operational rigor with technology intelligence. Their model focuses on solving real-world challenges rather than being a simple digital listing service, ensuring smoother and faster transactions for buyers and sellers alike.

Market Opportunity

India’s resale housing market is large but inefficient, characterised by fragmented intermediaries, inconsistent pricing, long transaction timelines, and low consumer trust. While primary real estate has undergone digitisation, the secondary market continues to lag behind.

Rising urbanisation, premiumisation, and growing consumer willingness to pay for convenience have created a significant opportunity for platforms that professionalise resale transactions. Investors see this segment as a high-potential, underpenetrated market within India’s broader proptech ecosystem.

Business Model and Revenue Streams

Truva operates on a transaction-led business model, earning revenue through service fees tied to successful resale deals. By managing the full transaction lifecycle—valuation, verification, negotiation, and closure—the company aligns its incentives with both buyers and sellers.

Central to its model is TruIQ, a proprietary real estate intelligence engine that uses over 1,000 data points, including floor plans, transaction history, walk-in demand, and micro-market trends, to provide accurate unit-level valuations. According to the company, homes listed on Truva sell 7–10 times faster than the market average, while ensuring transparent pricing and superior customer satisfaction.

Since launch, Truva has built a presence in seven micro-markets in Mumbai, facilitating resale transactions worth over ₹500 crore and serving more than 200 buyers and sellers, with 6x year-on-year growth.

Future Plans

Truva aims to expand into more than 20 micro-markets across Mumbai, Delhi NCR, Bengaluru, and other cities over the next 12 months. The startup targets ₹1,500 crore in gross merchandise value (GMV), driven by geographic expansion, enhanced operational efficiency, and continuous technology investment.

The company also plans to deepen its data-driven capabilities, further improve transaction speed and predictability, and establish itself as India’s most trusted resale homes brand.

Conclusion

With strong institutional backing, a differentiated full-stack model, and a clear focus on execution, trust, and technology, Truva is well-positioned to address long-standing inefficiencies in India’s resale housing market. The funding will help the company scale operations, expand geographically, and deepen technological capabilities, making it a leading player in India’s proptech ecosystem.

title: Reliance Jio (Jio Platforms): How a Bold Vision Transformed India’s Telecom and Digital Ecosystem
content:
Introduction

Every industry-defining company begins with a clear vision, and Reliance Jio’s journey is a powerful example of how long-term thinking, massive investment, and customer-centric innovation can reshape an entire nation’s digital future. Reliance Jio is India’s leading telecom and digital services company that revolutionized how millions of Indians access the internet. Launched commercially in 2016 under Reliance Industries, Jio transformed India from a data-scarce market into one of the world’s largest consumers of mobile data. Today, Jio Platforms stands at the center of India’s digital economy, powering connectivity, content, commerce, and cloud services.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

Reliance Jio was conceptualized and launched under the leadership of Mukesh Ambani, Chairman and Managing Director of Reliance Industries Limited. With decades of experience in building large-scale infrastructure businesses, Ambani envisioned broadband connectivity as essential national infrastructure—similar to roads, power, and water.

The idea behind Jio emerged from a key realization: while India had a rapidly growing population and increasing smartphone adoption, access to affordable high-speed internet remained limited. Data was expensive, speeds were inconsistent, and digital services were inaccessible to a large portion of the population. Mukesh Ambani believed that affordable internet could unlock economic growth, innovation, and social inclusion at an unprecedented scale.

 

The Existing Problem

Before Jio’s launch, India’s telecom and digital ecosystem faced several critical challenges:

• High data costs: Mobile internet was expensive, restricting usage to basic needs.
• Poor network quality: Inconsistent speeds and limited coverage impacted user experience.
• Low digital inclusion: Large sections of rural and semi-urban India lacked reliable internet access.
• Fragmented digital services: There was no integrated ecosystem for content, commerce, and connectivity.

Existing telecom players operated on legacy networks and pricing models that limited mass adoption. As a result, India’s digital potential remained largely untapped.

 

The Solution

Reliance Jio addressed these challenges with a bold, infrastructure-first strategy. Instead of upgrading older systems, Jio invested billions of dollars to build an all-IP, all-4G LTE network from the ground up, covering the entire country.

Key innovations included:

• Free voice calling and ultra-low-cost data plans, removing financial barriers for users.
• Nationwide 4G coverage, including Tier 2, Tier 3, and rural regions.
• Digital-first onboarding, making SIM activation and services seamless.
• Integrated digital apps ecosystem, including JioCinema, JioTV, JioSaavn, and JioCloud.

By focusing on affordability, scale, and reliability, Jio made high-speed internet accessible to millions of first-time users.

 

Early Challenges & Struggles

Launching Jio was not without challenges. The company faced heavy skepticism due to its massive capital expenditure and aggressive pricing strategy. Building nationwide infrastructure required regulatory approvals, spectrum investments, and large-scale execution. Additionally, incumbent telecom operators resisted the disruption, leading to intense competition and price wars.

Despite these challenges, Reliance Jio remained committed to its long-term vision. Continuous investment, operational discipline, and customer-centric execution helped Jio overcome early hurdles and rapidly scale its subscriber base.

 

Growth & Key Milestones

Once launched, Jio’s growth was unprecedented in the global telecom industry. Major milestones include:

• Acquiring over 100 million subscribers within six months of launch.
• Becoming India’s largest mobile network operator by subscriber base.
• Driving India to become the world’s largest mobile data consumer.
• Launch of Jio Platforms, consolidating digital services under one entity.
• Strategic investments from global leaders like Meta, Google, Silver Lake, KKR, and Intel in 2020.

These milestones validated Jio’s strategy and positioned it as a global digital powerhouse.

 

Business Model & Strategy

Jio operates on a diversified business model combining telecom services with digital platforms. Its revenue streams include:

• Mobile and broadband subscriptions.
• Digital content and entertainment platforms.
• Enterprise solutions, cloud services, and IoT.
• Digital commerce through JioMart.

Strategically, Jio focuses on:

• Affordable pricing to drive mass adoption.
• Building a full-stack digital ecosystem.
• Partnering with global technology leaders.
• Enabling small businesses and enterprises through digital tools.

This integrated approach allows Jio to scale efficiently while creating long-term value.

 

Impact & Recognition

Reliance Jio has had a transformative impact on India’s economy and society:

• Enabled digital inclusion for hundreds of millions of Indians.
• Accelerated adoption of online education, digital payments, and e-governance.
• Created millions of direct and indirect employment opportunities.
• Empowered startups, MSMEs, and content creators through connectivity.

Jio is widely recognized as a catalyst for India’s digital revolution and a benchmark for infrastructure-led innovation.

 

Current Status & Future Vision

Today, Jio continues to lead innovation in 5G deployment, AI-driven services, cloud computing, and digital commerce. Its long-term vision is to build a connected, intelligent, and inclusive digital India—supporting the next billion internet users and enabling future-ready technologies.

 

Conclusion & Takeaway

Reliance Jio’s success story proves that visionary leadership, patient capital, and infrastructure-led innovation can reshape entire industries. By solving India’s connectivity challenges at scale, Jio didn’t just build a telecom company—it built the foundation of a digital nation.

title: Dot & Key Clocks ₹423 Cr Operating Revenue in FY25 as Profitability and Scale Accelerate
content:
Introduction

Kolkata-based skincare brand Dot & Key delivered a standout performance in FY25, reporting ₹423 crore in operating revenue and ₹56 crore in net profit, marking a defining phase in its evolution from a niche D2C startup to one of India’s fastest-growing beauty brands. Founded in 2018 by Anisha Agarwal Saraf and Suyash Saraf, the company more than doubled its revenue year-on-year while significantly expanding margins—an uncommon feat in India’s highly competitive beauty and personal care market.

Backed by Nykaa, which now owns nearly 90% of the company, Dot & Key’s FY25 results underline the strength of its problem-led product strategy, disciplined cost structure, and growing dominance across online and offline channels.

 

Financial Performance in FY25

According to filings with the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA), Dot & Key reported operating revenue of ₹423.37 crore in FY25, up 113% from approximately ₹199 crore in FY24. Net profit after tax rose sharply to ₹56.09 crore, compared to ₹15.48 crore in the previous fiscal year.

The company’s PAT margin expanded from 7.7% in FY24 to 13.3% in FY25, reflecting strong operating leverage and improved pricing discipline. Industry estimates place Dot & Key’s EBITDA margin at around 14% for FY25, implying EBITDA of roughly ₹59–60 crore. The relatively small gap between EBITDA and PAT highlights the company’s asset-light model, low interest costs, and limited depreciation.

In addition to statutory revenue, Dot & Key reported a Net Sales Value (NSV) of approximately ₹529 crore in FY25, while Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) crossed ₹900 crore—cementing its position as Nykaa’s largest private-label brand.

 

From Early Experimentation to Scalable Brand

Dot & Key was launched in 2018 with an initial capital of around ₹1 crore from the founders’ personal savings. Unlike brands that launched with wide product portfolios, Dot & Key adopted a focused, problem-solution approach—building targeted skincare products for concerns such as acne, pigmentation, dehydration, and sun damage.

The early years were marked by experimentation and setbacks. Several formulations failed to resonate, packaging issues emerged, and customer feedback was often critical. Co-founder Anisha Agarwal Saraf later acknowledged these challenges publicly, emphasizing the importance of rapid iteration and learning from failure—an approach that eventually shaped the brand’s formulation-first philosophy.

 

Founders and Leadership

Anisha Agarwal Saraf brings deep domain exposure to the business. Her family operates Joy Cosmetics, one of India’s legacy skincare brands, giving her early insight into product formulation and brand-building. With academic training in chemistry and a master’s degree in food technology, she played a central role in product development and ingredient-led innovation.

Suyash Saraf complemented this with financial and operational expertise. Trained in real estate finance at Indiana University and in London, he focused on scaling digital sales, managing costs, and ensuring profitability discipline. His philosophy—“If you don’t make money every day, you don’t sleep at night”—has become synonymous with Dot & Key’s financially conservative growth approach.

 

Nykaa Acquisition and Strategic Support

A pivotal inflection point came in September 2021, when Nykaa acquired a 51% stake in Dot & Key for approximately ₹97 crore, just ahead of its IPO. This marked Nykaa’s first acquisition of a D2C brand and gave Dot & Key access to distribution scale, supply-chain efficiencies, and brand visibility.

In September 2024, Nykaa increased its stake to nearly 90% by investing an additional ₹256–265 crore, valuing Dot & Key at roughly ₹680 crore—up from about ₹190 crore in 2021. The move followed rapid revenue growth and Dot & Key’s emergence as Nykaa’s top-performing private brand.

Despite Nykaa’s majority ownership, Anisha and Suyash Saraf continue to manage day-to-day operations and retain the remaining 10% stake.

 

Cost Structure and Unit Economics

While Dot & Key has not disclosed a detailed FY25 cost breakup, industry benchmarks suggest spending is concentrated across raw materials, marketing, logistics, and talent. The sharp expansion in profit indicates tighter marketing efficiency, improved repeat purchase rates, and stronger average order values.

Sales through Nykaa’s platform, quick-commerce channels, and offline retail have helped reduce customer acquisition costs while maintaining volume growth. The brand’s repeat purchase rate is estimated at 35–40%, supporting margin expansion without excessive discounting.

 

Product Expansion and Market Position

Dot & Key has expanded its portfolio beyond core skincare into haircare, body care, sunscreens, and nutraceuticals through its sub-brand IKWI. Recent launches include sunscreen sticks, tinted sunscreens designed for Indian skin tones, barrier-repair toners, retinol-based night creams, and vitamin supplements focused on skin health.

In FY25, Dot & Key outperformed several competing science-led skincare brands in revenue terms, emerging as one of the largest digital-first beauty brands in India.

 

Offline Expansion and Growth Outlook

Offline currently contributes around 10% of Dot & Key’s sales, but this is a key focus area. The brand is now present in over 20,000 multi-brand retail outlets and more than 230 Nykaa stores across India. Management plans to deepen penetration in Tier II and Tier III cities through kiosks and expanded retail partnerships.

Looking ahead, Dot & Key aims to grow 2–3x by FY27 through a combination of offline expansion, new product launches, and category adjacencies. While no formal IPO timeline has been announced, Nykaa’s near-total ownership and Dot & Key’s profitability position it as a potential public-market candidate in the coming years.

 

Conclusion

Dot & Key’s FY25 performance reflects a rare combination in India’s D2C beauty space—rapid scale, sustained profitability, and disciplined execution. From a self-funded startup to Nykaa’s largest private-label brand, the company’s journey underscores the power of focused product strategy, founder-led execution, and operational rigor. As it scales further across channels and categories, Dot & Key is fast becoming a benchmark for profitable growth in India’s beauty ecosystem.

title: Nivaan Care Raises $7 Million in Series A Funding Led by Sorin Investments
content:
Introduction

India’s healthcare landscape is witnessing a growing demand for specialized, patient-centric care, especially in areas that have long remained fragmented. One such segment is chronic pain management, affecting millions across the country. Addressing this gap, Nivaan Care, a single-specialty interventional pain management clinic chain, has raised $7 million in a Series A funding round, marking a significant milestone in its journey to scale evidence-based, minimally invasive pain treatment across India.

 

About the Funding

The Series A round was led by Sorin Investments, with continued participation from existing investors W Health Ventures, Endiya Partners, and Rebright Partners. This latest fundraise follows Nivaan Care’s $4.25 million seed round in February 2025, which was led by Endiya Partners along with W Health Ventures. The continued backing from existing investors reflects strong confidence in the startup’s clinical model, execution capabilities, and long-term vision.

 

Use of Funds

Nivaan Care plans to deploy the fresh capital across three core areas. First, the company will expand its clinic network across multiple Indian cities, strengthening its physical presence in both metro and select satellite markets. Second, it aims to enhance clinical and operational infrastructure, ensuring standardized care delivery and scalable operations. Third, the funding will support continued innovation in minimally invasive pain management procedures, improving patient outcomes while reducing dependency on surgery.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2023, Nivaan Care operates single-specialty clinics dedicated to treating chronic pain conditions such as back pain, knee pain, neck pain, and joint-related disorders. Unlike traditional care pathways that oscillate between basic physiotherapy and invasive surgery, Nivaan positions itself as an evidence-led, non-surgical alternative focused on minimally invasive interventions. The company follows a multidisciplinary care model, integrating interventional pain physicians, physiotherapists, psychologists, nutritionists, and care coordinators to deliver personalized treatment plans.

 

Founders and Origin Story

Nivaan Care was founded by Nivesh Khandelwal (Co-founder & CEO) and Vishwas Singh (Co-founder & COO). Khandelwal previously served as COO at Ridge IVF, where he gained experience in scaling healthcare platforms, while Singh has held leadership roles at companies such as Tata 1mg and Shuttl. The founders identified chronic pain management as a large yet underserved market in India, with limited access to specialized interventional care. Their vision was to build a dedicated “middle layer” of care—bridging the gap between physiotherapy and surgery—through standardized clinics and clinical excellence.

 

Market Opportunity

Chronic pain affects nearly one in five adults in India, translating to an estimated 270 million people. Despite the scale of the problem, interventional pain management remains a nascent specialty in the country. India currently has fewer than 200 interventional pain clinics, compared to 4,000–5,000 clinics in the United States. This stark gap highlights the massive opportunity for organized, scalable players like Nivaan Care to build nationwide infrastructure and redefine pain treatment standards.

 

Business Model and Revenue Streams

Nivaan Care operates a clinic-led healthcare delivery model, generating revenue through patient consultations, diagnostic evaluations, and minimally invasive procedures such as radiofrequency ablation and platelet-rich plasma therapy. The company emphasizes standardized protocols, clinical outcomes, and patient experience, enabling repeat visits and long-term care relationships. Its full-stack model allows tight control over quality, pricing transparency, and operational efficiency, which is critical for scaling specialty healthcare services.

 

Traction and Current Presence

The startup currently operates clinics across Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Jaipur, and Lucknow, serving both metro and satellite markets. To date, Nivaan Care has completed over 40,000 patient consultations and conducted approximately 5,000 minimally invasive procedures. According to the company, a significant proportion of patients report noticeable pain reduction and improved daily functioning, reinforcing the effectiveness of its multidisciplinary approach.

 

Future Plans

With the Series A capital, Nivaan Care plans to enter new markets such as Bengaluru and further strengthen its operational backbone. Over the next 4–5 years, the company aims to scale from around 15 clinics to 60–75 centers nationwide, building India’s first large-scale platform for interventional pain management. The focus will remain on execution excellence, standardized systems, and seamless patient journeys across locations.

Conclusion

Nivaan Care’s Series A funding marks an important step toward addressing one of India’s most widespread yet under-served healthcare challenges. By combining clinical expertise, minimally invasive treatments, and a scalable clinic model, the startup is positioning itself as a category-defining player in interventional pain management. With strong investor backing and a clear expansion roadmap, Nivaan Care is well-placed to transform how chronic pain is treated across India.

title: Success Story "PharmEasy" : From a Small Health-Tech Idea to India’s Leading Online Healthcare Platform
content:
Introduction

Every transformative startup begins with a simple idea, and PharmEasy’s journey is a remarkable example of how solving real-world problems with technology, persistence, and customer focus can disrupt an entire industry. Founded in 2015, PharmEasy is India’s leading online pharmacy and healthcare platform, providing millions of users with access to medicines, diagnostic services, and healthcare support. What started as a small initiative to make medicines more accessible has grown into a trusted digital health ecosystem, reshaping how India manages healthcare. By combining convenience, affordability, and technology-driven efficiency, PharmEasy continues to expand access to quality healthcare across the nation.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

PharmEasy was founded by Dharmil Sheth and Dhaval Shah, two entrepreneurs who identified a critical gap in India’s healthcare system. They noticed that millions of patients, particularly in tier 2 and tier 3 cities, faced challenges in accessing essential medicines and diagnostic services. Travel to physical pharmacies or diagnostic centers was often time-consuming, and supply chains were inefficient, resulting in delayed or unavailable treatments. The founders believed that technology could bridge this gap, reduce inefficiencies, and make healthcare delivery faster, transparent, and affordable. Starting with online medicine delivery allowed them to address an immediate pain point, while laying the foundation for broader digital healthcare services, including lab tests, health tracking, and preventive care.

 

The Existing Problem

Before PharmEasy, accessing healthcare in India was cumbersome and fragmented. Patients often had to spend hours traveling to pharmacies, only to find medicines out of stock, or visit diagnostic labs for tests and collect reports days later. Many smaller towns lacked reliable healthcare services, and local pharmacies struggled with inventory management, procurement, and deliveries. Payment processes were often manual, adding another layer of inconvenience. Existing solutions failed to provide a seamless, end-to-end digital experience. This left millions underserved, particularly the elderly, busy professionals, and those living in remote areas. The founders recognized that this gap presented an opportunity to leverage digital technology to bring healthcare closer to the patient, creating a platform that could streamline the entire ecosystem.

 

The Solution

PharmEasy’s solution was to build a comprehensive digital healthcare platform that combined pharmacies, diagnostic labs, and health services into a single, convenient ecosystem. Users could order medicines online, schedule lab tests with home sample collection, and receive results digitally, all via a smartphone app. The platform also offered personalized reminders for refills and routine health checkups, promoting preventive care. By addressing both supply-side inefficiencies in pharmacies and diagnostics and the demand-side challenges faced by patients, PharmEasy created a solution that was simple, scalable, and highly effective. Its integrated approach ensured that users received reliable services quickly, while healthcare partners benefited from improved operations, inventory management, and delivery logistics.

 

Early Challenges & Struggles

PharmEasy’s early journey was far from smooth. Convincing local pharmacies and diagnostic labs to adopt a digital platform was challenging, as many were skeptical of technology and hesitant about online business models. Operational hurdles, such as coordinating deliveries across cities and ensuring accurate stock management, tested the team’s resilience. Funding was limited, requiring the founders to be resourceful in building partnerships and scaling operations. Additionally, educating customers to trust online healthcare services was crucial in a market unfamiliar with digital healthcare. The founders personally onboarded pharmacies, monitored deliveries, and refined the app experience, iterating continuously based on feedback. This hands-on approach built credibility and trust, establishing PharmEasy as a reliable partner in healthcare.

 

Growth & Key Milestones

Once credibility was established, PharmEasy experienced rapid growth. The platform expanded across India, reaching tier 2 and tier 3 cities, and partnered with thousands of pharmacies and diagnostic labs nationwide. Strategic funding from investors such as SoftBank, Temasek, and TPG Growth provided the capital needed to scale technology, enhance supply chains, and improve user experience. Monthly active users grew into the millions, with users relying on PharmEasy for medicine delivery, lab tests, and preventive care services. The company also pursued acquisitions and strategic partnerships, further expanding its service offerings and reinforcing its position as India’s leading health-tech platform.

 

Business Model & Strategy

PharmEasy operates as a marketplace and service aggregator, generating revenue through commissions on pharmacy and diagnostic services, subscription plans, and partnerships with healthcare providers. Its strategy focuses on convenience, reliability, and scalability. By investing in technology-led optimization, such as AI-driven inventory management and personalized app recommendations, PharmEasy ensures efficient operations and customer satisfaction. Its approach balances affordability for patients with sustainable growth for partner pharmacies and labs, making it a scalable and profitable business model.

 

Impact & Recognition

PharmEasy’s impact on India’s healthcare ecosystem has been profound. Millions of users now have easier access to medicines and diagnostics, particularly in underserved regions. Pharmacies and diagnostic labs have benefited from streamlined operations, technology support, and enhanced reach. By simplifying healthcare delivery, PharmEasy has encouraged preventive care, improved adherence to medication, and promoted digital adoption in healthcare. The company has received recognition as a leading health-tech platform and continues to be a benchmark for innovation, customer-centricity, and operational excellence in India’s healthcare sector.

 

Current Status & Future Vision

Today, PharmEasy continues to innovate, expanding teleconsultation, wellness services, and preventive healthcare offerings. Its vision is to become India’s most trusted digital health ecosystem, ensuring that quality healthcare is accessible, affordable, and convenient for all citizens. With ongoing investments in technology, logistics, and partnerships, PharmEasy is well-positioned to transform India’s healthcare landscape for years to come.

 

Conclusion & Takeaway

PharmEasy’s journey demonstrates that identifying real-world problems, solving them with innovative technology, and maintaining a customer-first mindset can create lasting impact. From a small health-tech startup to India’s leading online healthcare platform, PharmEasy proves that persistence, innovation, and trust are the foundations of industry-defining success. Behind every transformative startup is the right guidance, mentorship, and capital. title: Gully Labs Kicks Off Series A Round
content:
Introduction

India’s direct-to-consumer (D2C) fashion ecosystem continues to attract strong investor interest, particularly brands that combine design, culture, and community. One such brand is Gully Labs, a homegrown sneaker label rooted in Indian street culture, which has raised fresh capital to scale its operations and strengthen its presence in a competitive footwear market.

 

About the Funding

Sneaker brand Gully Labs has raised ₹26.5 crore (approximately $3 million) in a Series A funding round, led by Saama Capital, with participation from existing investor Zeropearl and several prominent angel investors. According to regulatory filings with the Registrar of Companies (RoC), the company issued 10 equity shares and 31,925 Series A compulsorily convertible preference shares (CCPS) at an issue price of ₹8,301 per share.

Saama Capital contributed ₹22.6 crore, while seed-stage backer Zeropearl invested ₹3.5 crore. The round also saw participation from Teppan, Roman Saini (Unacademy co-founder), Radhika Gupta (MD & CEO, Edelweiss Mutual Fund), and angel investor Aditya Bhalla. Post this round, Gully Labs is valued at approximately ₹147 crore ($16.25 million) on a post-money basis.

 

Use of Funds

The fresh capital will be utilised for business expansion and other corporate purposes, with a strong focus on scaling the brand. Gully Labs plans to expand its product portfolio, strengthen its offline retail presence in key Indian cities, and increase brand visibility in international markets, particularly the US and the UK. The company also intends to invest in operations, supply chain, and marketing to support its next phase of growth.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2023, Gully Labs operates a direct-to-consumer sneaker brand inspired by Indian street culture. The company designs and manufactures its footwear in-house, allowing it to maintain control over quality, storytelling, and brand identity. Gully Labs primarily sells through its own website and has also established a presence through select retail partners and physical stores in major Indian cities.

The brand differentiates itself through culturally inspired designs, including Phulkari-influenced sneakers and Onam-themed collections, blending premium materials with Indian narratives.

 

How It Started and the Founders

Gully Labs was co-founded by Arjun Singh and Animesh Mishra, who set out to build a sneaker brand that celebrates authentic Indian stories rather than imitating global trends. The founders believe that Indian pop and street culture remains underrepresented in global fashion, and Gully Labs aims to change that by creating products rooted in community, craftsmanship, and cultural pride.

Since inception, the brand has focused on building a strong identity around storytelling, quality, and originality—positioning itself as more than just a footwear label.

 

Market Opportunity

India’s sneaker and casual footwear market is witnessing rapid growth, driven by young consumers, rising disposable incomes, and increasing demand for homegrown D2C brands. The premium sneaker segment, in particular, remains under-penetrated, creating room for differentiated players like Gully Labs.

The brand competes with both domestic and global players, including Comet, Neemans, Solethreads, and Zeesh, while carving a niche through culture-led branding and limited-edition designs.

 

Business Model and Revenue Streams

Gully Labs operates a D2C-first business model, generating revenue through online sales and offline retail channels. Its in-house design and manufacturing capabilities help protect margins while enabling faster product experimentation. Since its seed round in early 2025, the company reports achieving ₹30 crore in annualised revenue and has already established physical stores in five Indian cities.

 

Future Plans

Looking ahead, Gully Labs aims to scale aggressively. The company plans to expand its retail footprint, diversify sneaker styles, and strengthen its international presence. Over the next few years, it is targeting ₹100 crore in annualised revenue by FY 2026–27, positioning itself as a leading premium sneaker brand from India.

 

Conclusion

Gully Labs’ Series A funding marks a key milestone in its journey to build a culture-driven, premium sneaker brand rooted in Indian identity. With strong investor backing, growing revenues, and a clear expansion roadmap, the startup is well-positioned to compete in India’s evolving D2C fashion landscape while taking its stories global.

title: Union Budget 2026–27: What It Means for Indian Startups, Tech Founders, and Investors
content:
Introduction to Union Budget 2026–27

Union Budget 2026–27 signals a clear shift in how the Indian government intends to support the technology and startup ecosystem. Instead of short-term incentives or startup-specific sops, the Budget lays down long-term structural foundations focused on predictable regulation, digital infrastructure, deeptech manufacturing, and an AI-ready workforce. For founders and investors, the message is unambiguous: the next phase of India’s startup growth will be driven by fundamentals, not freebies.

Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman framed “cutting-edge technologies, including AI applications” as force multipliers for governance and productivity, placing technology at the centre of India’s competitiveness agenda. While startups were mentioned only briefly in the speech, the policy moves around IT services, data centres, semiconductors, exports, and MSME financing will have a significant downstream impact on the ecosystem.

 

Clearer Tax Rules for IT and SaaS Companies

One of the most consequential announcements for tech companies is the simplification of transfer pricing rules. The Budget proposes to club software development services, IT-enabled services, KPO, and contract R&D under a single category—Information Technology Services—reflecting the reality of modern delivery models.

A common safe-harbour margin of 15.5% has been proposed, along with a sharp increase in the eligibility threshold from ₹300 crore to ₹2,000 crore. Crucially, approvals will be automated and rule-driven, without tax officer examination, and companies can continue under the same framework for five years.

For founders and CFOs, this reduces one of the most persistent sources of uncertainty while scaling globally. For investors, it lowers regulatory risk in IT services, SaaS, and Global Capability Centre (GCC) models, improving India’s attractiveness as a base for export-led tech businesses.

 

A Long Runway for Data Centres and Cloud Infrastructure

Budget 2026 makes a decisive push to position India as a global compute hub. Foreign companies providing cloud services globally using India-based data centres will be eligible for tax holidays until 2047, provided Indian customers are served through domestic reseller entities. A 15% safe-harbour provision on costs has also been proposed for related-party data centre services.

This move aims to crowd in long-duration global capital and reduce tax litigation in cross-border cloud structures. For AI-native startups—where compute costs dominate unit economics—this could reshape margins over time, although near-term gains may accrue more to hyperscalers. Still, policy certainty over two decades fundamentally improves India’s standing in the global cloud and AI infrastructure stack.

 

India Semiconductor Mission 2.0: From Assembly to IP

The Budget announced an expansion of the India Semiconductor Mission (ISM 2.0), alongside an increase in the outlay for the Electronics Component Manufacturing Scheme to ₹40,000 crore. The focus is shifting from assembly-led incentives to building an IP-centric ecosystem, including fabless design, OSAT units, and domestic supply chains.

For deeptech founders and investors, this opens long-term opportunities in chip design, semiconductor IP, and advanced manufacturing—critical enablers for AI, defence, and next-generation computing.

 

AI Push with an Employability Lens

While AI featured prominently, the Budget also acknowledged its impact on jobs. Sitharaman announced a High-Powered ‘Education to Employment and Enterprise’ Standing Committee to align skills, services exports, and workforce readiness with emerging technologies.

Linked to the target of achieving a 10% global share in services exports by 2047, this signals a shift away from volume hiring towards productivity, deployable skills, and continuous upskilling. For startups, workforce strategy is becoming a policy priority—not just an internal HR concern.

 

Practical Support for Startup Exports

One of the most founder-friendly changes is the removal of the ₹10 lakh per consignment cap on courier exports. This directly benefits D2C brands, maker-led startups, and small exporters that rely on frequent, lower-ticket international shipments. While understated, this reform removes a structural bottleneck that has long constrained cross-border e-commerce.

 

MSME Liquidity and Startup Supply Chains

Budget 2026 strengthens the financial infrastructure supporting MSMEs—many of which sit inside startup supply chains. A ₹10,000 crore SME Growth Fund has been proposed, along with a top-up to the Self-Reliant India Fund. Mandatory onboarding of CPSEs on TReDS, expanded invoice discounting, and plans for a secondary market for receivables aim to unlock liquidity and shorten payment cycles.

For venture-backed startups dependent on MSME vendors, these reforms address a chronic growth constraint: cash-flow mismatch despite demand.

 

Creators and the Orange Economy

Broadening its definition of the tech economy, the Budget highlighted the AVGC sector (Animation, VFX, Gaming, Comics), projected to require two million professionals by 2030. Support for AVGC creator labs across 15,000 schools and 500 colleges reflects recognition of content and creator-led platforms as serious economic drivers.

 

A Long-Game Budget for Serious Builders

From Angeltors’ perspective, Union Budget 2026–27 confirms a decisive shift from incentive-led growth to structure-led scaling. The government is not trying to boost startups in isolation; it is strengthening the rails that durable companies need—predictable taxation, deep digital infrastructure, skilled talent, and formal financial systems.

The expansion of safe-harbour rules materially reduces hidden regulatory risk in tech investing, improving outcomes for export-oriented and capital-efficient startups. The data centre push is a long-term bet on India’s role in the global compute economy, favouring founders who build for scale and discipline rather than short-term arbitrage.

Equally, the emphasis on employability over headcount aligns with what investors increasingly reward: lean teams, AI leverage, and measurable productivity.

At Angeltors, we see Budget 2026 as a long-game policy framework. The opportunity is significant—but it will favour founders who build strong fundamentals and investors who back substance over hype.
