title: Delhivery Reports ₹2,805 Crore Revenue in Q3 FY'26 as Profit Jumps 59%
content:
Introduction

India’s leading logistics and supply chain company Delhivery delivered a strong operational and financial performance in the third quarter of FY26, reporting robust revenue growth alongside a sharp improvement in profitability. The Gurugram-based firm posted an 18% year-on-year rise in operating revenue, while net profit surged 59%, reflecting the benefits of operating leverage, network optimisation, and disciplined cost management.

After reporting a loss in the previous quarter, Delhivery’s return to profitability highlights a clear shift toward sustainable growth. The company’s Q3 FY26 performance reinforces its position as a key backbone of India’s e-commerce and enterprise logistics ecosystem at a time when efficiency and margin discipline have become critical.

 

Financial Performance in Q3 FY26

According to filings with the National Stock Exchange (NSE), Delhivery’s revenue from operations increased to ₹2,805 crore in Q3 FY26, compared to ₹2,378 crore in Q3 FY25. Including ₹77 crore from non-operating income, total revenue for the quarter stood at ₹2,882 crore.

The company reported a net profit of ₹40 crore, up from ₹25 crore in the same quarter last year and a sharp turnaround from the ₹50 crore loss reported in the previous quarter. For the first nine months of FY26, Delhivery recorded operating revenue of ₹7,658 crore, with cumulative profit reaching ₹88 crore.

EBITDA more than doubled to ₹208.5 crore, while operating margin improved to 7.4%, up from 4.3% in Q3 FY25—signalling stronger unit economics and better cost absorption across the logistics network.

 

Cost Structure and Operational Efficiency

Freight handling and servicing costs continued to be the company’s largest expense, accounting for nearly 70% of total expenditure. These costs rose 7.4% year-on-year to ₹1,980 crore, significantly slower than revenue growth.

Other major expense components included:

  • Employee benefit expenses of ₹410 crore, down 4% YoY
  • Depreciation and amortisation of ₹187 crore
  • Legal, finance, and other overheads of ₹243 crore, up 4% YoY

The moderation in fixed and variable costs relative to revenue growth played a key role in driving profitability during the quarter.

 

Founders and Company Background

Founded in 2011 by Sahil Barua, Mohit Tandon, Kapil Bharati, and Suraj Saharan, Delhivery was built with the vision of creating a fully integrated, technology-first logistics network for India. What started as a hyperlocal delivery company has since evolved into one of the country’s most comprehensive logistics platforms, serving e-commerce, enterprises, SMEs, and emerging digital businesses.

The founders focused early on building proprietary technology, data-driven routing systems, and scalable infrastructure—capabilities that now form Delhivery’s competitive moat.

 

Business Expansion and Key Growth Segments

Delhivery’s core revenues continue to come from logistics services, including express parcel delivery, freight, warehousing, last-mile delivery, and logistics management systems.

A major operational highlight in Q3 FY26 was the performance of the Part Truck Load (PTL) business. PTL volumes grew 23% year-on-year, with the company crossing the 500,000 metric tonne milestone for the first time. Growth was driven by strong enterprise demand, consistent service quality, and expanding national coverage.

Over the years, Delhivery has expanded beyond e-commerce logistics into:

  • Integrated warehousing and supply chain solutions
  • B2B freight and PTL services
  • Cross-border logistics and data-driven logistics software

This diversification has helped the company reduce dependence on any single segment and improve revenue stability.

 

Funding History and Investor Support

Delhivery is backed by marquee global investors, including SoftBank Vision Fund, Tiger Global, Fosun, Carlyle, and Steadview Capital. The company raised over $1.4 billion prior to its IPO and went public in 2022, becoming one of India’s largest listed logistics players.

In Q3 FY26, Delhivery also granted 70,900 employee stock options (ESOPs) worth approximately ₹3 crore, underlining its focus on long-term talent retention and ownership-led culture.

 

Regulatory Impact and Leadership Update

During the quarter, Delhivery assessed the impact of India’s newly notified Labour Codes, resulting in an increase of ₹20.86 crore in gratuity and leave-related liabilities. This amount was classified as an exceptional item in the company’s financials.

Separately, the company announced that Chairman Deepak Kapoor stepped down from its board after serving since 2017, having contributed to Delhivery’s strategic growth phase.

 

Market Position and Outlook

At the close of the last trading session, Delhivery’s shares traded at ₹426.35, translating to a market capitalisation of ₹31,903 crore (approximately $3.54 billion).

Looking ahead, Delhivery is well-positioned to benefit from rising e-commerce penetration, increased enterprise outsourcing of logistics, and structural improvements in supply chain efficiency. With profitability returning and margins expanding, the company appears to be transitioning from a scale-first strategy to a more disciplined, sustainable growth model.

Delhivery’s Q3 FY26 results signal growing maturity—where scale, efficiency, and profitability are beginning to move in sync.

title: Success Story "boAt" : From a Bootstrapped Accessories Startup to India’s Audio & Wearables Leader
content:
Introduction

Every iconic consumer brand begins by solving a simple, everyday problem—and boAt’s journey is a powerful example of how deep customer insight can create a category-defining company. Founded in 2016, boAt is today India’s leading audio and wearables brand, known for blending style, performance, and affordability. What started as a small, bootstrapped venture selling durable charging cables has grown into a lifestyle technology powerhouse used by millions across the country. By understanding Indian consumers better than global competitors, boAt reshaped the audio market and built a brand that strongly resonates with Gen Z and millennials.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

boAt was founded by Aman Gupta and Sameer Mehta, entrepreneurs with hands-on experience in consumer electronics and distribution. Aman Gupta, in particular, noticed a recurring frustration among Indian consumers—charging cables and earphones broke frequently, forcing users to repeatedly spend money on replacements. Despite high demand, there were very few brands offering durable, good-looking accessories at affordable prices. The founders realized that Indian consumers didn’t want “cheap” products; they wanted value for money with style. This insight became the foundation of boAt’s mission: to create lifestyle electronics designed for real Indian usage, without premium pricing.

 

The Market Gap

Before boAt entered the scene, India’s accessories and audio market was divided into two extremes. On one side were cheap imports with poor quality and zero brand trust. On the other were expensive international brands priced beyond the reach of most consumers. There was no strong Indian brand catering to the growing youth population that wanted performance, aesthetics, and affordability together. At the same time, rising smartphone usage and content consumption were increasing demand for personal audio devices. boAt identified this “missing middle” and positioned itself squarely to own it.

 

The Product Strategy

boAt began by solving a basic but widespread pain point—fragile charging cables. Its early products focused on durability, reinforced materials, and everyday reliability. Once customer trust was established, boAt expanded into earphones and headphones, tuning sound output for bass-heavy preferences popular among Indian listeners. Over time, the company diversified into TWS earbuds, smartwatches, speakers, and lifestyle wearables. Instead of competing on cutting-edge tech alone, boAt focused on delivering aspirational design, solid performance, and accessible pricing—turning functional gadgets into fashion-forward accessories.

 

Early Challenges & Bootstrapped Growth

boAt’s early journey was not without challenges. Competing against well-funded global brands while operating on limited capital required sharp execution and constant learning. Managing supply chains, ensuring consistent quality, and building brand awareness were ongoing struggles. The founders relied heavily on customer feedback, quickly fixing product issues and improving designs. A digital-first approach allowed boAt to test products faster, control costs, and scale efficiently. This disciplined execution helped boAt grow organically while building strong customer loyalty.

 

Growth, Scale & Market Leadership

As India’s digital adoption accelerated, boAt’s growth surged. The company leveraged e-commerce platforms and social media to reach consumers across the country, including Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities. Strategic celebrity collaborations with artists like Diljit Dosanjh and leading Indian cricketers helped boAt connect deeply with youth culture. Over time, the brand built a loyal community of users known as “boAtheads,” transforming customers into brand ambassadors.

By 2023, boAt became the No. 1 brand in India’s hearables category and ranked as the world’s fifth-largest wearable brand by volume. As of 2025, boAt commands nearly 38.5% of India’s wearables market, a remarkable achievement in a space dominated by global giants.

 

Revenue, Funding & Financial Performance

boAt’s financial performance reflects its strong market position and disciplined strategy. In FY 2024–25, the company reported an 18% year-on-year revenue growth, crossing ₹4,000 crore in annual revenue. Growth was driven by strong demand across TWS earbuds, smartwatches, and audio accessories. boAt has raised strategic capital from investors such as Warburg Pincus and Malabar Investments, enabling expansion of its product portfolio, supply chain, and brand presence, while maintaining a clear focus on profitability.

 

Business Model & Brand Strategy

boAt operates on a digital-first, D2C-led model, selling primarily through online marketplaces and its own platform, supported by selective offline retail. This approach allows faster product launches, efficient inventory management, and direct consumer engagement. Rather than competing on discounts, boAt focuses on storytelling, influencer marketing, and rapid product innovation. The brand positions itself not just as a tech company, but as a lifestyle brand that blends music, fitness, and fashion.

 

Impact & Future Vision

boAt has proven that Indian brands can compete at scale by combining consumer insight with strong branding and execution. It has generated large-scale employment across manufacturing, logistics, and marketing, while inspiring a new generation of Indian D2C startups. Looking ahead, boAt aims to expand globally, invest in connected ecosystems, and strengthen its position in smart wearables, while staying true to its core promise of affordability and style.

 

Conclusion

boAt’s journey shows that solving everyday problems with clarity, consistency, and customer obsession can build industry-leading companies. From a bootstrapped accessories startup to India’s dominant audio and wearables brand, boAt stands as a blueprint for building scalable consumer businesses in India.

title: The Whole Truth Raises $51 Mn in Series D, Marks Start of IPO Journey
content:
Introduction

Mumbai-based clean-label nutrition startup The Whole Truth (TWT) has raised approximately $51 million in a Series D funding round, marking a major milestone as the company sharpens its focus on profitability and prepares for an eventual public market listing.

The round was led by Sofina and Sauce.vc and comprised a mix of primary and secondary capital. It also saw participation from existing investors Peak XV Partners, Rainmatter Health, and AYRA Ventures. Early backers of the company include Z47 (formerly Matrix Partners India) and prominent angel investors such as Sriharsha Majety (Swiggy) and Jaydeep Burman (Rebel Foods).

 

Capital Deployment and IPO Readiness

According to the company, the freshly raised capital will be deployed towards scaling in-house manufacturing capacity, strengthening working capital, and building internal systems, governance structures, and compliance frameworks required to become “public market ready”.

A report noted that the Series D round marks the formal beginning of The Whole Truth’s IPO journey, with profitability identified as the next critical milestone on its roadmap.

“The Whole Truth has proven that a brand built on 100 percent truth can scale across categories,” said Shashank Mehta, Founder and CEO of The Whole Truth. “The next question is—can we do all this profitably, while holding the highest possible bar on quality and honesty? This round marks the start of that journey.”

 

Founders and Product Philosophy

Founded in 2019 by Shashank Mehta, a former Unilever executive, The Whole Truth operates in the clean-label packaged food and nutrition segment. The brand was built to address a growing trust deficit in packaged foods by eliminating hidden sugars, artificial flavours, preservatives, and misleading claims from product labels.

Rachna Aggarwal, co-founder of the company, leads product development and formulation, playing a central role in shaping the brand’s innovation pipeline and clean-label standards.

The company’s product portfolio includes protein bars, protein powders, nut butters, peanut butter, and dark chocolates, targeted at health-conscious consumers seeking transparency and functional nutrition.

One of its recent launches—super-light protein powders—targets under-penetrated consumer segments such as women, teenagers, and the elderly, expanding beyond the traditional gym-focused protein audience and significantly widening its addressable market.

 

Growth Milestone

The Whole Truth had earlier raised $15 million in a Series C funding round in February last year, led by Sofina with participation from Z47, Peak XV Partners, and Sauce.vc.

Since its Series C round in January 2025, the company claims to have grown threefold, driven by expanding distribution, higher repeat consumption, and strong brand recall. The startup now commands leading market share across several key product categories, according to industry estimates.

Financially, the growth has been sharp. The company’s revenue from operations surged 232% to ₹216 crore in FY25, compared to ₹65 crore in FY24. During the same period, losses widened marginally to ₹28 crore in FY25, up from ₹24 crore in FY24, reflecting continued investments in brand building, manufacturing capabilities, and supply chain expansion.

 

Riding India’s Protein Opportunity

The Whole Truth’s growth comes amid a broader “proteinification” trend in India. According to data from the UN Food and Agriculture Organization, India’s daily per capita protein supply stood at 70.5 grams in 2021, significantly lower than China (124.6 grams) and the US (124.3 grams).

A 2024 IMRB survey found that over 90% of Indians are unaware of their daily protein requirements, while 73% of urban diets fall short of recommended protein intake.

India’s protein supplement market, valued at around $1.4 billion in 2024, is projected to grow to $1.8 billion by 2029, according to Mordor Intelligence. This widening nutrition gap has created a strong opportunity for clean-label brands positioned around trust, education, and functional foods.

 

Competitive Landscape and Distribution

The Whole Truth operates in a competitive ecosystem alongside brands such as Yoga Bar (ITC-backed), OZiva, Wellbeing Nutrition, SuperYou, and Max Protein.

The company follows an omnichannel distribution strategy, selling through its direct-to-consumer (D2C) platform, major e-commerce marketplaces, quick-commerce platforms, and offline retail stores across urban India.

Quick-commerce platforms such as Blinkit and Zepto have played a key role in accelerating adoption of “better-for-you” products, where clean-label and functional foods often command 15–25% price premiums.

 

Investor Confidence and Long-Term Outlook

Investors backing the company remain confident in its long-term potential.

“We are proud to partner Shashank and the team from day zero to their Series D, as they fix broken consumer trust in food,” said Manu Chandra, founder of Sauce.vc.

Yana Kachurina, principal at Sofina, added, “The Whole Truth has built a brand rooted in honesty, quality, and a deep understanding of the consumer.”

 

The Road Ahead

As The Whole Truth enters its next phase of growth, the company plans to focus on new category launches, selective long-term partnerships, deeper direct-to-consumer engagement, and disciplined cost and margin management.

With strong revenue momentum, rising consumer awareness around protein, and a clear path toward profitability, The Whole Truth is positioning itself as one of India’s most credible clean-label nutrition brands preparing for the public markets.

The Whole Truth’s journey highlights how strong founder-market fit, clean branding, and operational discipline can build scalable consumer brands in emerging categories.

title: BlackBuck Posts ₹32 Cr Profit in Q3 FY'26 as Scale and Operating Leverage Drive Turnaround
content:
Introduction

Bengaluru-based online trucking platform BlackBuck Limited has reported a strong financial turnaround in Q3 FY26, posting a profit of ₹32 crore on ₹172 crore in operating revenue. The quarter marked a significant milestone for the company as it moved from losses in the previous year to sustained profitability, driven by scale, operating leverage, and growing adoption of its digital trucking services.

Founded to organise India’s fragmented trucking ecosystem, BlackBuck has steadily evolved from a load-matching platform into a full-stack logistics and fleet-services marketplace. Its latest performance reflects how scale, product diversification, and cost discipline are beginning to converge into a profitable growth model.

 

Financial Performance

According to financial statements filed with the National Stock Exchange (NSE), BlackBuck’s revenue from operations grew 51% year-on-year to ₹172 crore in Q3 FY'26, compared to ₹114 crore in the same quarter last year. Including ₹16 crore in interest income, the company’s total income stood at ₹188 crore, up from ₹123 crore in Q3 FY'25.

For the nine-month period ended December 2025, BlackBuck reported revenue of ₹466.5 crore, representing a 53% increase over ₹305 crore in the corresponding period last year.

Profitability improved sharply, with the company reporting a net profit of ₹32 crore in Q3 FY'26, compared to a loss of ₹48 crore a year earlier. On a sequential basis, profit increased 10% from ₹29 crore in Q2 FY'26. EBITDA for the quarter stood at ₹44.77 crore, while adjusted EBITDA was ₹50.04 crore, reflecting improving operating leverage.

 

From the Beginning to Now: BlackBuck’s Journey

BlackBuck was founded with the objective of digitising India’s highly fragmented trucking and freight market. In its early years, the company focused on solving basic inefficiencies such as load discovery, payment delays, and lack of transparency for truck operators.

Over time, BlackBuck expanded beyond a pure marketplace model. It began building services aimed directly at truck owners and fleet operators, gradually positioning itself as a technology-led infrastructure provider for trucking. This evolution allowed the company to deepen engagement with its user base while creating multiple monetisation avenues.

Today, BlackBuck’s business is anchored in serving truck operators through a suite of digital tools rather than just facilitating freight transactions. This shift has been central to its ability to scale revenues while improving margins.

 

Founders and Leadership

BlackBuck is led by Rajesh Yabaji, Co-founder and CEO, who has played a key role in shaping the company’s long-term strategy. Under his leadership, BlackBuck has transitioned from a growth-at-all-costs approach to one focused on profitability and sustainable scale.

During Q3 FY'26, Yabaji sold 20 lakh shares, representing 9.37% of his holding, in a ₹135 crore bulk deal. Following the transaction, his stake declined from 11.81% to 10.7%, out of the company’s total 18.07 crore equity shares. Despite the partial exit, management continues to signal confidence in BlackBuck’s long-term growth trajectory.

 

Cost Structure

On the cost side, BlackBuck reported total expenditure of ₹142 crore in Q3 FY'26, compared to ₹93 crore in Q3 FY'25.

  • Employee benefit expenses rose 16% year-on-year to ₹40.5 crore, accounting for approximately 29% of total costs.

  • Depreciation and other operating expenses formed the remaining major overheads, reflecting ongoing investments in technology, platform infrastructure, and service expansion.

While costs increased in absolute terms, margin expansion indicates better cost absorption and improving unit economics as the business scales.

 

Product Expansion and Market Position

BlackBuck’s revenue continues to be dominated by truck operator services, which contributed nearly 98% of operating revenue in Q3 FY'26. These services include load discovery, freight payments, telematics, and related digital tools.

Beyond its core offerings, the company has seen strong traction across newer segments:

  • Telematics recorded record product sales during the quarter.

  • Superloads, a growth category, reported over 270% year-on-year growth.

  • Vehicle finance disbursals increased 35% quarter-on-quarter, highlighting rising adoption of credit solutions among truck operators.

This diversification strengthens BlackBuck’s position as a full-stack digital platform within India’s logistics ecosystem.

 

Expansion and Growth Outlook

Looking ahead, BlackBuck plans to focus on expanding market share across its core trucking services while scaling growth businesses such as Superloads and vehicle finance in a calibrated manner. The company is also investing in deeper digitisation of freight through classifieds and marketplace tools.

Management has indicated that operating leverage from core businesses will be used to fund new initiatives, reducing reliance on aggressive capital deployment. With consistent revenue growth and improving profitability, BlackBuck appears well-positioned to sustain momentum through the remainder of FY'26.

 

Conclusion

BlackBuck’s Q3 FY'26 performance marks a clear inflection point—from a loss-making logistics startup to a profitable, scaled digital trucking platform. Strong revenue growth, expanding margins, and growing adoption across products signal increasing maturity in the company’s business model.

As India’s logistics sector continues to digitise, BlackBuck’s ability to combine scale with sustainable unit economics will be critical to its long-term success.

title: EyeROV Raises ₹13 Crore in Pre-Series A Funding to Scale Marine Robotics Innovation
content:
Introduction

India’s deep-tech startup ecosystem is steadily gaining momentum as investors increasingly back companies solving complex, real-world infrastructure and defence challenges through advanced engineering. Among these emerging players is EyeROV, a Kochi-based marine robotics startup operating in underwater inspection and defence technology. With rising demand for safer, data-driven alternatives to manual underwater inspections, EyeROV has raised fresh capital to accelerate innovation, scale deployments, and strengthen its global footprint.

 

About the Funding

Marine robotics startup EyeROV (IROV Technologies Pvt. Ltd.) has raised ₹13 crore ($1.44 million) in a pre-Series A funding round, co-invested by AWE Funds and Unicorn India Ventures. The investment reflects growing confidence in India’s deep-tech capabilities, particularly in sectors such as marine robotics, defence technology, and critical infrastructure inspection.

According to the company, this round serves as a strategic precursor to a planned $10 million Series A, for which EyeROV has already begun seeing early interest from both Indian and global investors. The funding follows strong commercial traction, including a recent ₹47 crore order from the Indian Navy, reinforcing the startup’s credibility in defence and government-led projects.

 

Use of Funds

The newly raised capital will be deployed to accelerate research and development, support new product innovation, and expand EyeROV’s presence across India and international markets. The company plans to strengthen its engineering and AI capabilities, improve robotic autonomy, and scale deployments across sectors such as energy, ports, offshore infrastructure, and defence.

EyeROV will also invest in enhancing manufacturing capabilities and operational readiness to support larger and more complex inspection mandates, particularly from government and enterprise customers.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2017, EyeROV operates at the intersection of marine robotics, infrastructure inspection, and defence technology. The startup develops advanced Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) and Unmanned Surface Vessels (USVs) designed for challenging underwater environments where traditional manual diving is risky, costly, and inefficient.

Its robotic systems integrate non-destructive testing (NDT) payloads, including ultrasonic testing, sonar imaging, and AI-based inspection tools, enabling high-accuracy inspections, real-time reporting, and digital twin creation for large-scale assets such as dams, bridges, offshore pipelines, ports, and subsea cables. EyeROV’s proprietary long-tunnel inspection capability of up to 10 kilometres places it among a select group of global players with this expertise.

 

How It Started and the Founders

EyeROV was co-founded by Johns T. Mathai and Kannappa Palaniappan P with the vision of building indigenous underwater robotics solutions from India for global infrastructure, energy, and defence needs. The founders identified a critical gap in safe, scalable, and data-driven underwater inspection technologies, especially as aging offshore and subsea assets require frequent monitoring and regulatory compliance.

From the outset, EyeROV focused on deep engineering, proprietary technology development, and close collaboration with large industrial, defence, and government clients, allowing it to build systems tailored to real operational challenges rather than purely experimental use cases.

 

Market Opportunity

EyeROV operates within the global underwater infrastructure inspection market, driven by over $1 trillion worth of aging offshore and subsea assets that require regular inspection and maintenance. The company estimates a total addressable market exceeding $13 billion globally, with India accounting for approximately $800 million.

Rising investments in ports, offshore energy, defence modernisation, climate-resilient infrastructure, and disaster preparedness continue to expand demand for robotic inspection solutions. As safety, efficiency, and sustainability become priorities, autonomous and electric marine robotics are increasingly replacing manual inspection methods worldwide.

 

Business Model and Revenue Streams

EyeROV follows a hybrid business model, offering both product sales and Robotics-as-a-Service (RaaS) to B2B and B2G customers. This approach enables clients to access advanced robotics without high upfront capital expenditure while allowing EyeROV to build recurring and long-term revenue streams.

The company has executed over 150 projects for more than 80 clients across power, oil and gas, ports, shipping, infrastructure, marine research, and defence. Its client base includes major industrial players and government organisations such as the Indian Navy, DRDO, and the Indian Coast Guard, underlining its strong positioning in mission-critical applications.

 

Future Plans

Looking ahead, EyeROV plans to scale internationally while deepening its footprint in India’s energy and defence sectors. The company aims to expand its product portfolio, enhance AI-driven inspection capabilities, and strengthen its presence in global markets with high offshore infrastructure density.

With increasing demand for autonomous, electric, and safer inspection systems, EyeROV is positioning itself as a globally competitive marine robotics player, aligned with long-term trends in infrastructure resilience and defence modernisation.

 

Conclusion

EyeROV’s pre-Series A funding marks a significant milestone in its journey to build world-class marine robotics technology from India. With strong investor backing, proven commercial traction, and a large global market opportunity, the startup is well-positioned to lead innovation in underwater inspection and defence robotics.

title: How Juspay Built the Backbone of India’s Payment Ecosystem
content:
Introduction

Every transformative startup begins by solving a problem that others overlook. Juspay’s journey is a powerful example of how focusing on core infrastructure rather than consumer visibility can create outsized impact. Founded in 2012, Juspay has grown into one of India’s most critical fintech companies, quietly powering the country’s digital payments ecosystem. In 2026, the Bengaluru-based startup reached a landmark milestone by becoming India’s first unicorn of the year, achieving a valuation of $1.2 billion while also turning profitable.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

Juspay was founded by Vimal Kumar, Srinivas Ananth, and Naveen Kumar, engineers with deep expertise in large-scale systems and enterprise technology. While working with high-traffic digital platforms, the founders noticed a recurring issue: payments frequently failed at checkout, leading to lost revenue and poor customer experiences. Despite rapid growth in digital adoption, the underlying payments infrastructure was fragile and fragmented.

The founders believed that India needed a robust, developer-friendly, and scalable payments layer that businesses could trust. Instead of building another consumer-facing fintech app, they chose to focus on infrastructure—creating systems that would remain invisible to users but indispensable to businesses.

 

The Core Market Challenge

As India’s digital economy expanded, payment failures became a systemic bottleneck. Businesses faced high transaction drop-offs due to unreliable routing, inconsistent UPI performance, and slow checkout flows. Existing solutions emphasized payment options rather than reliability, leaving large platforms struggling to scale efficiently.

For high-volume companies, even a small improvement in success rates translated into significant revenue gains. This gap presented Juspay with a clear opportunity to become the backbone of digital payments.

 

Juspay’s Infrastructure-Led Approach

Juspay built a full-stack payments infrastructure platform focused on reliability, performance, and security. Products like Express Checkout and HyperSDK optimized transaction routing, reduced failures, and improved conversion rates across UPI, cards, and wallets.

By integrating deeply into merchant systems and prioritizing compliance, Juspay positioned itself as a long-term infrastructure partner rather than a short-term service provider. This approach helped the company earn trust among India’s largest digital platforms.

 

Early Challenges and Long-Term Vision

Building infrastructure is capital-intensive and slow to monetize. In its early years, Juspay operated with limited visibility and faced competition from consumer-focused fintech startups with aggressive growth strategies. Revenue growth was gradual, and profitability remained elusive.

However, the founders stayed committed to their long-term vision. Instead of chasing rapid expansion, they invested in engineering excellence, system reliability, and customer trust. This patience laid the foundation for sustainable scale.

 

Scale, Profitability, and Unicorn Status

By 2025–26, Juspay had reached massive operational scale. The company now processes over 300 million transactions daily and handles more than $1 trillion in annualised Total Payment Volume. Its infrastructure powers payments for major platforms including Amazon, Flipkart, and Swiggy, as well as key UPI and banking partners.

In FY25, Juspay achieved a significant milestone by turning profitable, reporting a net profit of ₹62 crore. This marked a decisive shift from high-growth losses to sustainable financial performance. In January 2026, a $50 million funding round led by WestBridge Capital, with participation from Kedaara Capital, valued the company at $1.2 billion, making Juspay India’s first unicorn of 2026. The startup is also backed by SoftBank, reinforcing strong investor confidence.

 

Business Model and Strategy

Juspay operates on a B2B SaaS and infrastructure revenue model, earning through transaction-based pricing, platform usage fees, and value-added services. Its strategy emphasizes deep integrations, long-term enterprise partnerships, and continuous optimization rather than short-term margins.

This disciplined approach has allowed Juspay to balance scale with profitability while remaining resilient in a competitive fintech landscape.

 

Global Expansion and Future Vision

With fresh capital, Juspay is expanding across Asia-Pacific, Europe, the Middle East, and the Americas. Its long-term ambition is to become a global payments infrastructure provider, offering reliable and scalable systems for digital economies worldwide.

 

Conclusion and Takeaway

Juspay’s journey demonstrates that lasting success is built by solving foundational problems with patience and precision. By prioritizing infrastructure, reliability, and trust, Juspay has transformed from a behind-the-scenes startup into a profitable unicorn shaping the future of digital payments.

Behind every such success story is the right mix of vision, capital, and mentorship. Angeltors connects ambitious founders with experienced angel investors who bring not just funding, but strategic guidance to help startups scale sustainably.

If you are building the next infrastructure-led success or looking to invest in India’s future-defining startups, Angeltors is where long-term value meets opportunity.

title: Varaha Raises $20 Million in Series B Tranche to Scale High-Integrity Carbon Removal Projects
content:
Introduction

India’s climate-tech ecosystem is rapidly emerging as a global force, with startups addressing some of the world’s most pressing environmental challenges through science-led innovation. One such company is Varaha, a Gurugram-based carbon removal project developer, which has raised fresh capital to accelerate the scale and global reach of its climate solutions. The funding marks a significant milestone as Varaha strengthens its position in the fast-evolving carbon removal and climate infrastructure space.

 

About the Funding

Varaha has raised $20 million in the first tranche of its ongoing Series B funding round, led by WestBridge Capital, as part of a larger round in which the company aims to raise approximately $45 million. Existing investors RTP Global and Omnivore also participated in the round with follow-on investments, reaffirming their confidence in Varaha’s long-term vision and execution capabilities.

This investment marks WestBridge Capital’s first climate-tech investment, highlighting growing institutional interest in science-backed, scalable climate solutions emerging from India.

 

Use of Funds

The newly raised capital will be deployed to accelerate Varaha’s geographic expansion, strengthen its scientific and Measurement, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) capabilities, and scale a newly launched industrial partnership model. The company aims to extend its carbon removal platform to global partners while maintaining high standards of integrity, transparency, and scientific validation.

A key focus will be scaling Varaha’s proprietary systems that enable accurate carbon measurement and verification—an increasingly critical requirement as scrutiny of voluntary carbon markets intensifies worldwide.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2022, Varaha is a carbon removal project developer operating across multiple nature-based and technology-enabled pathways. The company develops projects across biochar, afforestation, reforestation and revegetation (ARR), regenerative agriculture, and enhanced rock weathering (ERW)—four of the most credible and scalable approaches to long-term carbon dioxide removal.

Headquartered in Gurugram, Varaha currently operates over 20 carbon removal projects across India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Bhutan, and the Ivory Coast, positioning it as one of Asia’s largest developers of high-integrity carbon removal credits.

 

How It Started and the Founders?

Varaha was co-founded by Madhur Jain, who serves as CEO and Co-Founder, Ankita Garg (Co-founder and COO), Vishal Kuchanur (Co-founder and CTO), with a mission to build a science-first, globally credible carbon removal platform from India. The founding vision centred on solving two parallel challenges: removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere at scale while strengthening smallholder farmer livelihoods through climate-positive practices.

Since inception, Varaha has focused heavily on scientific rigor, digital verification, and transparent methodologies—areas often criticised within the voluntary carbon market. This emphasis has helped the company build credibility with global buyers seeking durable, high-quality carbon removal credits.

 

Market Opportunity

Varaha operates within the rapidly expanding global carbon removal market, driven by increasing net-zero commitments from large corporations and governments. Demand is shifting away from short-term offsets toward durable, high-integrity carbon removal solutions that deliver measurable climate impact.

The company’s long-term ambition is to help sequester one billion tonnes of CO₂e, primarily through smallholder farmer-led projects. With climate regulations tightening and corporate sustainability goals becoming more stringent, the addressable market for verified carbon removal is expected to grow significantly over the next decade.

 

Business Model and Commercial Traction

Varaha has built strong commercial momentum through long-term carbon offtake agreements with global buyers. Notably, the company has signed agreements with Microsoft for carbon credits generated from its biochar projects in India, along with similar deals involving Google and a major US-based aviation company.

These multi-year agreements provide predictable revenue visibility and validate Varaha’s approach to combining scientific integrity with commercial scalability. The company has already delivered one of the highest volumes of durable carbon removal credits globally among project developers.

 

Varaha Industrial Partners Program (VIPP)

As part of its next phase of growth, Varaha has launched the Varaha Industrial Partners Program (VIPP)—a biochar-focused partnership model designed to scale carbon removal through industrial collaboration. Under VIPP, industrial operators with gasification capabilities and access to sustainable biomass can partner with Varaha to generate verified carbon removal credits.

Varaha provides end-to-end support, including digital MRV systems, sensor installation, third-party verification, and carbon credit origination. The resulting credits can be sold as offsets or used as insetting credits to support partners’ own decarbonisation goals. The program is already operational, with projects underway in West Africa, India, and with a major Indian steel company.

 

Investor Perspective

Investors backing the round highlighted Varaha’s rare combination of deep scientific credibility and a commercially viable business model. WestBridge Capital noted that the company is uniquely positioned to build a global carbon removal platform from India, capable of delivering integrity, scale, and long-term impact.

Existing investors RTP Global and Omnivore also reiterated their conviction in Varaha’s leadership and execution, citing strong demand signals from global enterprise customers.

 

Conclusion

Varaha’s $20 million Series B tranche marks a defining moment in its journey to build one of the world’s most credible carbon removal platforms. With robust science, strong commercial traction, and expanding global partnerships, the company is well-positioned to play a meaningful role in shaping the future of climate infrastructure.

As demand for verified, durable carbon removal accelerates globally, Varaha stands out as a climate-tech company combining impact, scale, and integrity—built from India for the world.

title: HUL-Backed Minimalist Crosses ₹500 Cr Revenue in FY25, Posts Loss Amid ₹46 Cr Exceptional Charge
content:
Introduction

Hindustan Unilever Limited (HUL)-owned direct-to-consumer skincare brand Minimalist has crossed a major milestone in FY25, with revenue surging 48% year-on-year to ₹514.8 crore. The Jaipur-based science-led beauty brand has now entered the ₹500 crore revenue club, reinforcing its position as one of India’s fastest-growing premium D2C skincare companies.

However, despite strong topline growth and positive operating performance, the company reported a net loss during the fiscal year due to one-time exceptional expenses. The numbers highlight both the scale Minimalist has achieved and the cost intensity of building a high-growth digital-first beauty brand.

 

Financial Performance

Minimalist’s revenue from operations rose sharply to ₹514.8 crore in FY25, up from ₹347.4 crore in FY24, according to consolidated financial statements filed with the Registrar of Companies (RoC). Including ₹2.84 crore in non-operating income, total income stood at ₹517.6 crore, compared to ₹350.1 crore in the previous fiscal year.

Despite strong revenue growth, the company reported a net loss of ₹31.5 crore in FY25, compared to a net profit of ₹10.8 crore in FY24. The loss was primarily driven by exceptional items worth ₹46.1 crore recorded during the year.

Importantly, before accounting for these one-time charges and taxes, Minimalist posted a profit of ₹13.6 crore, indicating that core operations remained stable.

The company reported an EBITDA of ₹18 crore, with an EBITDA margin of 3.45%, while Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) stood at 10.55%. On a unit economics basis, Minimalist spent ₹0.98 to earn every ₹1 of operating revenue, demonstrating near break-even efficiency at scale.

 

From Beginning Till Now: How They Began

Founded in 2020 in Jaipur by Mohit Yadav and Rahul Yadav, Minimalist entered the Indian beauty market with a sharp differentiation strategy — transparency, science-backed formulations, and ingredient-led positioning.

At a time when consumers were increasingly aware of skincare ingredients, Minimalist positioned itself as a brand offering actives like niacinamide, salicylic acid, retinol, and vitamin C in clean, minimal packaging — similar to global ingredient-focused brands.

The company adopted a digital-first, D2C model, selling through its own website and major online marketplaces such as Amazon, Nykaa, and Flipkart.

Within just five years, the brand scaled rapidly, riding on India’s growing premium skincare demand and the surge in online beauty commerce.

 

Founders and Leadership

Minimalist was founded by Mohit Yadav and Rahul Yadav, who collectively control 62% of the company. Their product-led and digital-first approach helped the brand gain rapid consumer trust and scale.

Before the HUL acquisition, Minimalist raised $17 million in funding, including a $15 million Series A round led by Peak XV, which holds a 27.9% stake in the company.

In January 2025, FMCG giant Hindustan Unilever Limited (HUL) signed an agreement to acquire a 90.5% stake in Minimalist at a pre-money valuation of ₹2,955 crore (approximately $350 million). The deal is among the largest transactions in India’s D2C beauty segment and is expected to close in Q1 FY26.

 

Cost Structure

As Minimalist scaled, expenses grew alongside revenue. Total expenses rose 51% year-on-year to ₹504 crore in FY25, compared to ₹333.2 crore in FY24.

Key cost components included:

  • Advertising & Promotion: ₹154 crore (over 30% of total expenses), up 28% YoY.
  • Cost of Materials Consumed: ₹146.7 crore, up 57% YoY.
  • Distribution Costs (Marketplace Commissions): ₹84.3 crore.
  • Employee Benefits: ₹36.8 crore, up 29% YoY.
  • Other Overheads (Rent, Warehousing, Logistics, Legal): ₹82 crore.

The sharp increase in marketing and marketplace commissions reflects continued investments in customer acquisition, brand building, and distribution expansion.

 

Product Expansion and Market Position

Minimalist offers a range of skincare and haircare products, including serums, toners, moisturizers, cleansers, and active-based treatments. Product sales remained the sole source of operating revenue in FY25.

The brand has carved a niche in India’s premium skincare segment by combining affordability with science-backed positioning. Its transparent ingredient communication strategy and strong marketplace ratings have helped it compete effectively against both domestic D2C brands and international beauty players.

Crossing ₹500 crore in revenue places Minimalist among the leading digitally native beauty brands in India.

 

Expansion and Growth Outlook

With the HUL acquisition, Minimalist gains access to deep distribution networks, supply chain efficiencies, and financial backing from one of India’s largest FMCG players.

While FY25 reflected a temporary setback due to one-time exceptional charges, core operating performance remained positive. The integration with HUL is expected to strengthen offline distribution, improve sourcing efficiencies, and support long-term brand building.

As consumer demand for premium, ingredient-focused skincare continues to rise, Minimalist is well-positioned to expand both online and offline channels, scale margins, and deepen market penetration.

The absence of visible operational disruption post-acquisition suggests that HUL is allowing the brand autonomy — a positive sign for sustained innovation and growth.

 

Conclusion

Minimalist’s FY25 performance highlights the dual reality of high-growth D2C brands — rapid scale with significant cost intensity. While exceptional expenses pushed the company into a reported loss, its strong revenue growth, positive EBITDA, and strategic backing from HUL signal long-term potential.

With ₹500+ crore revenue, strong brand recall, and FMCG backing, Minimalist stands at a crucial inflection point in India’s premium beauty market.

title: Success Story "Mamaearth" : Building India’s First Toxic-Fee Beauty Unicorn
content:
Introduction

Every iconic consumer brand begins by solving a deeply personal: Indian parents lacked access to safe, toxin-free baby care products. What started as a small, digitally problem. For Mamaearth, that problem was safety. Founded in 2016, Mamaearth emerged from a simple but powerful realization native brand focused on baby skincare has since grown into one of India’s most successful beauty and personal care companies. In 2022, Mamaearth became India’s first beauty and personal care unicorn, redefining how modern Indian consumers view skincare, trust, and brand transparency.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

Mamaearth was founded by Varun Alagh and Ghazal Alagh, a husband-and-wife duo based in Gurugram. The inspiration behind the brand was deeply personal. When the couple struggled to find safe, chemical-free baby products for their own child, they realized that Indian markets were flooded with products containing toxins, parabens, and harmful chemicals.

Rather than importing products or compromising on quality, they decided to build their own toxin-free brand. Their mission was clear: create natural, safe, and certified products designed specifically for Indian consumers and Indian skin types.

 

The Market Gap

At the time Mamaearth launched, India’s beauty and baby care market was dominated by legacy FMCG giants. While these brands had strong distribution, transparency about ingredients was limited. Globally, clean beauty was gaining momentum, but India lacked a strong, trustworthy, toxin-free brand that spoke directly to millennial parents.

Simultaneously, India was witnessing the rise of digital-first consumers. Young buyers were researching ingredients online, reading reviews, and demanding safer options. Mamaearth positioned itself at the intersection of clean beauty and digital commerce, targeting millennials who valued transparency and authenticity.

 

Digital-First & Direct-to-Consumer Strategy

Unlike traditional FMCG brands that relied heavily on offline retail, Mamaearth adopted a digital-first, direct-to-consumer model. Social media marketing, influencer partnerships, and performance-driven advertising became its primary growth engines.

The brand focused on storytelling—educating consumers about harmful chemicals while highlighting its “Made Safe” certification. This transparency helped build strong trust with young parents and skincare enthusiasts. Over time, Mamaearth expanded beyond baby care into hair care, skincare, and color cosmetics, broadening its consumer base significantly.

 

Product Expansion & Brand Portfolio

Mamaearth’s early success in baby care allowed it to scale into adjacent categories such as onion hair oil, vitamin C serums, face washes, and sunscreens. The brand capitalized on trending natural ingredients while maintaining affordability.

As demand grew, Mamaearth’s parent company, Honasa Consumer Pvt. Ltd., adopted a house-of-brands strategy. It expanded its portfolio by launching and acquiring other beauty brands, strengthening its position in India’s rapidly growing personal care market.

 

Funding, Growth & Unicorn Status

Mamaearth attracted significant investor interest early in its journey. The company raised funding from prominent investors including Sequoia Capital India (now Peak XV Partners) and Fireside Ventures. In 2022, Mamaearth achieved unicorn status with a valuation exceeding $1 billion, becoming India’s first beauty and personal care unicorn.

In 2023, Honasa Consumer successfully went public through an IPO, marking a major milestone in its growth journey. The public listing validated Mamaearth’s strong brand equity, revenue growth, and scalable business model.

 

Revenue & Financial Performance

Mamaearth’s growth has been driven by strong revenue expansion and increasing brand recognition. The company scaled rapidly through online marketplaces like Amazon and Flipkart while strengthening its offline retail presence across India.

Its asset-light manufacturing model and strong gross margins enabled sustainable scaling. Over time, Mamaearth moved from a high-growth startup phase toward improving operational efficiency and long-term profitability.

 

Challenges & Competitive Landscape

The beauty and personal care market is highly competitive, with both established FMCG giants and emerging D2C brands competing for attention. Rising customer acquisition costs and increasing competition in digital advertising posed challenges.

However, Mamaearth’s early-mover advantage in clean beauty, strong brand recall, and omnichannel strategy helped it maintain leadership. Its ability to continuously launch new products based on consumer trends allowed it to stay relevant.

 

Impact & Industry Influence

Mamaearth played a key role in popularizing the clean beauty movement in India. It shifted consumer conversations toward ingredient transparency and toxin-free formulations. The brand inspired a wave of D2C startups in personal care, accelerating innovation in the sector.

Beyond business impact, Mamaearth generated large-scale employment and built a modern Indian beauty brand with global aspirations.

 

Future Vision

Looking ahead, Mamaearth aims to expand internationally, strengthen its offline distribution network, and grow its house-of-brands portfolio. By leveraging data-driven product development and strong consumer insights, the company is positioning itself for long-term leadership in the beauty industry.

 

Conclusion

Mamaearth’s journey proves that deeply personal problems can spark billion-dollar businesses. By combining authenticity, digital execution, and consumer trust, Varun and Ghazal Alagh built a brand that reshaped India’s beauty landscape.

title: Indigrid Technology Raises ₹40 Crore to Close Extended Series A Round
content:
Introduction

India’s electronics manufacturing and embedded systems landscape is gaining traction as capital flows into companies that combine deep engineering with scalable production. A key player emerging from this wave is Indigrid Technology, a Gurugram-based integrated electronics manufacturing company that has secured ₹40 crore in fresh funding, bringing the total size of its extended Series A round to ₹75 crore. The successful close reflects investor confidence in India’s ability to build sophisticated manufacturing platforms serving both domestic and global OEMs.

 

About the Funding

The recently concluded tranche of funding was led by WestBridge Capital, with participation from Valour Capital, ITI Growth Opportunities Fund, Vimson Group, and Global South Capital. This round follows an earlier strategic infusion of $4 million (approximately ₹35.2 crore) from existing lead investor Cactus Partners in September last year, further solidifying the long-term backing of the company’s institutional partners.

The extended Series A close represents a major milestone for Indigrid, enabling it to accelerate its growth trajectory in high-growth segments such as automotive, electric mobility, industrial automation, and consumer electronics.

 

Use of Funds

Indigrid plans to deploy the fresh capital toward expanding manufacturing capacity, strengthening research and development capabilities, supporting working capital needs, and exploring strategic acquisitions that can further enhance its value chain. As embedded systems become more complex with increased integration of electrification, automation, and advanced controls, investment in design and technology becomes a key competitive advantage.

A portion of the capital will be used to enhance operations at the company’s existing facilities and to accelerate setup of larger production units, particularly in Goa, where Indigrid has acquired land for future expansion.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2015 by Sameer Narang and Rishab Puri, Indigrid Technology develops and manufactures embedded electronic systems for a wide range of sectors. The company is positioned as an integrated partner across the electronics value chain, offering capabilities in EMS (Electronics Manufacturing Services), ESDM (Electronic System Design & Manufacturing), and ODM (Original Design Manufacturing).

Indigrid’s product suite includes motor controllers, DC-DC converters, and regulator rectifiers for automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs). In addition, the company produces control systems for consumer appliances and battery pack assemblies used in electric vehicles and drones, illustrating its relevance across both traditional and emerging technology domains.

 

How It Started and Founders’ Vision ?

Indigrid Technology was co-founded by Sameer Narang and Rishab Puri, who recognized the need for end-to-end electronics manufacturing expertise in India — a market that has traditionally been dominated by imports, especially in precision embedded systems. Their vision was to build a vertically integrated platform that could not only handle manufacturing at scale but also design, prototype, test, and deliver complex systems with global quality standards.

Under their leadership, Indigrid has steadily expanded its capabilities, moving beyond basic contract manufacturing to become a technology-led production partner for customers seeking reliability, quality, and scale.

 

Market Opportunity

India’s electronics sector is undergoing rapid transformation, driven by the government’s push toward domestic manufacturing, rising demand for EVs, industrial automation, and consumer IoT devices. This macro-tailwind, coupled with global supply chain realignment, presents a significant opportunity for companies like Indigrid that possess both design and manufacturing prowess.

The global embedded systems market is projected to grow significantly over the next decade, and localization of key components — especially in the automotive and mobility space — is becoming a priority for global OEMs. India’s positioning as a “China-plus-one” manufacturing hub further enhances the relevance of integrated platforms like Indigrid.

 

Business Model and Revenue Streams

Indigrid operates a hybrid business model that includes design services, prototype engineering, full-scale manufacturing, testing, and post-production support. Its integrated platform allows customers to reduce time-to-market and improve quality control by consolidating multiple stages of electronics product development under one roof.

The company currently serves over 35 marquee customers, ranging from domestic OEMs to global enterprises, reinforcing its broad industry relevance. Its products and assemblies are used in a variety of applications, from automotive controls and EV battery systems to industrial controllers and consumer appliances.

 

Operational Footprint and Expansion Plans

Indigrid currently operates three advanced manufacturing facilities across Manesar and Goa, with the recent commissioning of an additional unit in Goa and land acquisition for future expansion. The three facilities are equipped with state-of-the-art production lines, quality control labs, and testing infrastructure that meet global standards.

With the new funds, the company plans to further scale capacity, deepen its technology stack, and evaluate M&A opportunities that align with its long-term growth strategy. These steps are expected to strengthen Indigrid’s position as a leading integrated electronics manufacturer capable of serving both Indian and international markets.

 

Investor Confidence and Strategic Backing

Investors backing this round have underscored Indigrid’s strong execution and potential to capture a growing share of the electronics manufacturing opportunity.

Karan Goshar, Managing Partner of Valour Capital, highlighted that Indigrid is building critical capabilities for India’s industrial resilience, especially as global companies diversify supply chains. Mohit Gulati, Managing General Partner at ITI Growth Opportunities Fund, noted that Indigrid’s technology focus aligns well with the fund’s investment thesis in high-growth electronics sectors.

Mragank Jain, Managing Partner at Global South Capital, emphasized that Indigrid’s growth trajectory positions it to capture the manufacturing localisation and global expansion opportunity. Meanwhile, Rajeev Kalambi, General Partner at Cactus Partners, pointed to the company’s execution excellence and governance as key reasons for continued support.

 

Conclusion

Indigrid Technology’s successful closing of its extended Series A round at ₹75 crore marks a defining moment in its growth journey. With robust investor backing, expanding manufacturing footprint, and deepening technology capabilities, the company is poised to become a cornerstone of India’s electronics manufacturing ecosystem.

As demand for embedded systems grows across automotive, EVs, industrial, and consumer sectors, Indigrid’s integrated platform and strategic investments position it to play a leading role in India’s shift toward advanced electronics production.

title: GIVA Crosses ₹500 Cr Revenue Mark in FY'25 Amid Aggressive Expansion
content:
Introduction

Bengaluru-based jewellery startup GIVA delivered another year of strong growth in FY25, reinforcing its position as one of India’s fastest-growing new-age jewellery brands. The company’s operating revenue surged 89% year-on-year to ₹518 crore, up from ₹274 crore in FY24. However, rapid expansion across categories and retail formats came at a cost, as losses widened during the same period.

 

Financial Performance

According to filings with the Registrar of Companies (RoC), GIVA’s total income stood at ₹523 crore in FY25. Despite the impressive topline growth, the company reported a net loss of ₹72 crore, a 22% increase from ₹59 crore in FY24.

Profitability pressures stemmed largely from heavy investments in store expansion, marketing, and talent. Still, there were signs of improving efficiency: GIVA spent ₹1.15 to earn every rupee in FY25, better than ₹1.23 in FY24. EBITDA margin improved to –10.81%, while ROCE stood at –21.52%.

 

From Silver Startup to Multi-Category Brand

Founded as a digital-first silver jewellery brand, GIVA initially focused on affordable, everyday designs targeted at young consumers. Over time, the company expanded into gold jewellery and lab-grown diamonds, moving up the value chain while retaining its contemporary design philosophy.

Revenue is now almost evenly split between online and offline channels, highlighting the brand’s successful omnichannel strategy.

 

Founders and Leadership

GIVA is led by founder and CEO Ishendra Agarwal, Nikita Prasad (Creative Head), and Sachin Shetty, They have steered the company from a niche online startup to a nationally recognized jewellery brand. Under his leadership, GIVA has prioritized scale, brand visibility, and retail expansion to compete with both traditional jewellers and new-age D2C players.

 

Cost Structure

Rapid growth significantly increased expenses. The cost of materials — primarily silver, gold, and diamonds — rose 97% to ₹227 crore, accounting for 38% of total spending. Inventory levels doubled to ₹100 crore due to higher procurement.

Employee expenses jumped 82% to ₹91 crore as the company expanded teams across retail, operations, and corporate functions. Marketing spend rose 55% to ₹135 crore, reflecting aggressive brand-building efforts. Rental costs surged 135% to ₹47 crore due to store expansion. Overall expenses climbed 76% to ₹596 crore.

 

Product Expansion and Market Position

GIVA’s shift beyond silver into gold and lab-grown diamonds has broadened its customer base and improved average order value. The company crossed 200 stores during FY25 and is approaching 300 outlets, giving it one of the widest offline footprints among new-age jewellery brands.

Internationally, GIVA entered Sri Lanka with its first overseas store, generating ₹10.7 crore in revenue. Its nationwide presence now rivals established players such as BlueStone and CaratLane, the latter operating under Titan Company Limited.

 

Expansion and Growth Outlook

To date, GIVA has raised about $122 million in funding, with Creaegis leading a $61.5 million Series C round and IQ Capital among key investors. The company is preparing for a public listing once revenue reaches an annual run rate of ₹1,800–2,000 crore, which management expects within two to three years.

However, external factors such as rising gold and silver prices and margin pressures in lab-grown diamonds could influence future performance.

 

Conclusion

GIVA’s journey highlights a classic startup trade-off: aggressive growth versus short-term profitability. By building a strong omnichannel brand, expanding categories, and pursuing national scale, the company has positioned itself for long-term leadership in India’s modern jewellery market.

For investors, GIVA represents the evolving face of consumer brands — digital-first origins, offline scale, and IPO ambitions. As the company moves toward profitability and public markets, it will be a closely watched story in India’s retail landscape.

title: Success Story "Ather Energy" :  Powering India’s Electric Mobility Revolution
content:
Introduction

Every transformative startup begins with a bold vision, and Ather Energy’s journey shows how deep technology, sustainability, and long-term thinking can reshape an entire industry. Founded in 2013 by Tarun Mehta and Swapnil Jain, Ather Energy set out to build electric scooters that were not just eco-friendly alternatives but superior machines in performance, intelligence, and design. What began as a student project at IIT Madras has evolved into one of India’s most premium and technology-driven EV brands, playing a pivotal role in the country’s transition toward clean mobility.

 

Founder Background & Idea Origin

Tarun Mehta and Swapnil Jain were passionate about engineering and sustainability. While studying at IIT Madras, they recognized that most electric scooters available in India were slow, unattractive, and technologically outdated. Instead of simply electrifying existing designs, they envisioned building a product from scratch — one that could outperform petrol scooters while delivering a smart, connected riding experience.

Their goal was clear: create an electric vehicle that urban consumers would aspire to own, not just tolerate for environmental reasons. This insight shaped Ather’s premium positioning from the very beginning.

 

The Existing Gap in the Market

When Ather started, India’s EV ecosystem was still in its infancy. Consumers faced several barriers: limited performance, unreliable battery technology, lack of charging infrastructure, and concerns about durability. Electric two-wheelers were often perceived as low-quality substitutes rather than genuine upgrades.

Additionally, traditional manufacturers were cautious about investing heavily in EV innovation, leaving a gap for a technology-first startup to redefine expectations. Ather identified this opportunity to build not just vehicles, but a complete mobility ecosystem.

 

The Solution: A Premium Smart EV Ecosystem

Ather launched its first product, the Ather 450, in 2018, followed by upgraded versions like the 450X. These scooters introduced features rarely seen in two-wheelers at the time: rapid acceleration, touchscreen dashboards with navigation, over-the-air software updates, smartphone connectivity, and advanced battery management systems.

Equally important was the creation of Ather Grid — a fast-charging network designed to reduce range anxiety and enable convenient urban travel. By controlling both the product and infrastructure, Ather ensured a seamless ownership experience.

 

Early Challenges & Struggles

Building a hardware-intensive startup in India is capital-intensive and complex. Ather faced challenges in manufacturing, supply chain development, regulatory approvals, and consumer education. Convincing buyers to switch from petrol to electric required overcoming skepticism about range, performance, and resale value.

The founders also had to invest heavily in research and development before generating significant revenue, making funding crucial for survival.

 

Funding Journey & Investor Support

Ather’s progress was made possible by strong backing from global investors and strategic partners. Early investments came from Tiger Global Management, followed by major support from Hero MotoCorp, which became its largest shareholder and manufacturing partner. Additional backing from Sachin Bansal and venture debt from InnoVen Capital further strengthened the company’s financial position.

In total, Ather has raised over $400 million across funding rounds, enabling expansion in production capacity, retail presence, and technology development.

 

Growth & Revenue Momentum

As fuel prices rose and environmental awareness increased, demand for electric mobility surged. Ather expanded rapidly into multiple cities, particularly urban centers where performance and charging access mattered most. Its manufacturing facility in Hosur, Tamil Nadu, allowed the company to scale production while maintaining quality control.

Rather than chasing mass-market volumes immediately, Ather focused on high-value customers, building a strong brand associated with innovation and reliability. This strategy helped the company achieve steady revenue growth and strong customer loyalty.

 

Competitive Landscape

India’s electric two-wheeler market has become intensely competitive, with players such as Ola Electric, TVS Motor Company, and Bajaj Auto entering the segment. However, Ather’s differentiation lies in its technology-first approach, premium positioning, and robust charging ecosystem.

While some competitors prioritize aggressive pricing and scale, Ather emphasizes performance, software innovation, and long-term customer experience.

 

Business Model & Strategy

Ather generates revenue primarily through vehicle sales, subscription-based software services, accessories, and charging solutions. The company’s direct-to-consumer retail model allows better control over brand experience, pricing, and after-sales service.

Its long-term strategy includes expanding into new segments, improving battery efficiency, and building deeper integration between hardware and software — effectively positioning itself as a mobility technology company rather than just a vehicle manufacturer.

 

Path Toward IPO

Ather Energy successfully went public in 2025, marking a historic moment for India’s clean-tech and EV ecosystem. The IPO included both a fresh issue of shares and an offer for sale, with funds allocated toward expanding manufacturing capacity, accelerating R&D, enhancing battery technology, and scaling the Ather Grid charging network. The listing demonstrated strong investor confidence in premium electric two-wheelers and significantly strengthened Ather’s position in the fast-growing EV market. It also boosted credibility against competitors and legacy manufacturers, signaling the maturity of India’s EV sector.

From a campus project to a publicly listed company, Ather’s journey represents one of India’s most important climate-tech success stories.

 

Impact & Future Vision

Ather Energy has contributed significantly to India’s clean-energy transition. It has helped normalize electric mobility, encouraged infrastructure development, and demonstrated that EVs can be aspirational products. Beyond environmental benefits, the company has created jobs across engineering, manufacturing, retail, and services.

Looking ahead, Ather aims to make electric mobility mains

tream while continuing to innovate in battery technology, connectivity, and sustainable transportation solutions.

 

Conclusion & Takeaway

Ather Energy’s journey proves that solving real problems with deep technology and long-term commitment can redefine entire industries. From a college project to a national EV leader preparing for public markets, the company exemplifies how innovation, patient capital, and strategic execution can build enduring value.

As India accelerates toward an electric future, Ather stands as a symbol of homegrown innovation capable of competing on a global stage — not just by following trends, but by shaping them.

title: Peptris Raises ₹70 Crore in Series A to Accelerate AI-Driven Drug Discovery
content:
Introduction

India’s deep-tech and healthcare innovation ecosystem is witnessing a surge in investor confidence, especially in startups using artificial intelligence to solve complex scientific problems. One such emerging player is Peptris, a Bengaluru-based drug discovery company leveraging AI to transform how new medicines are discovered and developed. As pharmaceutical companies worldwide face rising costs, long timelines, and high failure rates, AI-driven platforms are increasingly viewed as the future of drug development. Peptris has now secured fresh capital to accelerate its research pipeline and move promising therapies closer to clinical trials, positioning itself as a key player in next-generation healthcare innovation.

 

About the Funding

Peptris has raised ₹70 crore (approximately $7.7 million) in a Series A funding round co-led by IAN Alpha Fund and Speciale Invest. The round also saw participation from Tenacity Ventures, BYT Ventures, and other investors. This funding underscores growing global interest in AI-led drug discovery platforms capable of significantly reducing research timelines and improving success rates. Investors are increasingly backing companies that combine scientific rigor with scalable technology, and Peptris fits squarely within this category.

 

Use of Funds

The newly raised capital will be deployed over the next 24 months to advance the company’s existing drug programs toward clinical readiness while expanding its discovery pipeline. Peptris plans to strengthen its multidisciplinary teams across biology, chemistry, artificial intelligence, data science, and computational modeling. The startup also intends to build business development capabilities in the United States and Europe to deepen partnerships with global pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies. These investments aim to accelerate the transition from early research to clinical development — a critical milestone for any drug discovery company.

 

About the Startup

Founded in 2019, Peptris focuses on addressing one of healthcare’s most persistent challenges — the slow, expensive, and failure-prone nature of drug discovery. Traditionally, bringing a new drug to market can take more than a decade and cost billions of dollars, with the majority of candidates failing during early research stages. Peptris seeks to dramatically improve this process using AI-driven molecular discovery, predictive analytics, and computational modeling to identify viable drug candidates faster and with greater accuracy.

 

How It Started and the Founders

Peptris was co-founded by Narayanan Venkatasubramanian, Shridhar Narayanan, Anand Budni, and Amit Mahajan — professionals with deep expertise in computational science, artificial intelligence, and pharmaceutical research. Their vision was to harness machine learning and data-driven approaches to reduce uncertainty in early-stage drug development and address critical unmet medical needs worldwide. By combining expertise across technology and life sciences, the founding team built a platform designed to bridge the gap between computational innovation and real-world healthcare impact.

 

Technology and Market Opportunity

Peptris has developed proprietary AI models capable of generating novel molecules and predicting key drug development parameters such as efficacy, toxicity, stability, and manufacturability at a very early stage. This capability is particularly valuable in pre-clinical research, where many drug candidates fail due to unforeseen issues.

The platform has already produced Novel Chemical Entities (NCEs) and identified drug repurposing opportunities, including “rescue” programs involving shelved clinical-stage drugs from other pharmaceutical companies. By targeting bottlenecks in early discovery, Peptris aims to significantly shorten development timelines and improve success rates. With global demand rising for faster and more cost-efficient drug development, the market opportunity for AI-driven platforms is substantial.

 

Business Model and Revenue Streams

Peptris operates primarily on a B2B engagement model. It collaborates with pharmaceutical companies, biotechnology firms, and select FMCG partners to license discovered assets or co-develop new therapies. Revenue is generated through milestone payments, licensing fees, research partnerships, and potential royalties from successful drug commercialization.

The startup is currently focused on therapeutic areas with significant unmet needs, including rare diseases, oncology, inflammation, and women’s health — segments where traditional drug development has struggled to deliver sufficient solutions. By targeting these high-impact areas, Peptris aims to create both commercial value and meaningful patient outcomes.

 

Future Plans

Looking ahead, Peptris plans to initiate several new NCE programs alongside multiple drug repurposing initiatives. The company aims to advance multiple candidates into clinical trials, expand global partnerships, and position itself as a leading AI-driven drug discovery platform emerging from India. Strengthening its presence in international markets will be a key priority as it seeks collaborations with major pharmaceutical companies and research institutions. Over time, Peptris aspires to build a robust pipeline of therapies that can address some of the world’s most challenging diseases.

 

Conclusion

Peptris’ ₹70 crore Series A funding marks a significant milestone in its journey to transform drug discovery through artificial intelligence. With strong investor backing, a powerful technology platform, and a focus on high-impact therapeutic areas, the company is well positioned to contribute meaningfully to global healthcare innovation while building a scalable deep-tech business from India. As AI continues to reshape the life sciences sector, startups like Peptris could play a pivotal role in bringing faster, safer, and more effective treatments to patients worldwide.
